title: BrainStem: Open‑source bio‑inspired AI with 12 neuromodulators for learning
description: A neuro-symbolic cognitive architecture that transforms text corpora into a probabilistic relational world model using an SQLite backend. A homeostatic loop of 12 digital neurotransmitters autonomo...
fullText:
BrainStem is a biologically inspired, neuro-symbolic cognitive architecture for lifelong learning. It is designed to learn models of the structures and dynamics of language and text through context hypotheses, uncertainty, contradiction, revision, neuromodulation, replay, and consolidation rather than by merely storing isolated facts.

YouTube - BrainStem Project AI conversation 22.07.26

NotebookLM codebase exploration 22.07.26

BrainStem is a research and calibration system, not a production-ready assistant. Permanent fact, relation, and question writes remain locked while the learning core and its candidate flow are being validated.

BrainStem has completed the original Stage A foundation work and the Legacy Cleanup large slices A–C. Historical and unused learning paths were removed, including the obsolete nlp.py path, the legacy CorpusReader / Phase-3d word-role and filter path, and the historical Phase-4def–4p cluster. The current runtime uses the canonical Phase-7cort top path.

Additional completed foundation work includes:

The GUI chunk counter is the canonical corpus-read progress indicator. Counts of distinct context_hypotheses.chunk_id values are treated only as hypothesis-coverage proxies and not as an exact processed-chunk counter.

The Modern Gap Candidate Bridge and non-productive Phase-5f Shadow Observation path remain installed in the untouched canonical runtime.

Both Candidate and Observation frontiers reached the full current hypothesis population. Initial Shadow Observation generation completed without projection errors. The original V1 observation identity mixed stable identity with source_updated_at, causing repeated observations of the same hypothesis to appear as new keys instead of versions of one stable identity.

Stable Observation Identity V2 is installed as a parallel, non-productive dual-write path.

The first production parity cycle proved exact V1/V2 input parity. During the subsequent natural long run, V2 recorded:

A dedicated driver audit proved that all 136,990 recorded source-state changes were caused only by source_updated_at. The stored source content and the projection fingerprint remained unchanged. This established that the timestamp-sensitive V2 source fingerprint was technically consistent but too sensitive for semantic versioning.

A parallel Content-Stable Fingerprint Shadow Classifier is installed without changing the existing V2 writer.

The Classifier currently has no production events because the normal Observation checkpoint is fully caught up and no new context_hypotheses or Candidate Shadow source row exists beyond it. Fifty additional system cycles were verified as zero-input cycles for this path.

Post-corpus replay activity continues after all corpus chunks have been read. Phase 6a creates replay candidates and replay events, but its existing context_hypotheses update writes only replay-control and replay-provenance fields. It does not update semantic hypothesis fields and does not update the canonical context_hypotheses.updated_at field.

A future-only, context_hypotheses-scoped Replay-Control Shadow Capture is installed.

After correction of the installer hook to use the actual SQLite connection receiver db, the natural production test recorded exactly:

Each of the 60 observed hypotheses received five replay events, with one initial state and four stable refreshes. This proves natural runtime reachability, exact event parity, retry-safe identity and stable replay-control classification. It does not prove semantic learning.

Phase 6a currently computes outcome_observation_available from the global row count of phase5g_experiment_outcomes. It does not join by replay event, hypothesis identity or post-replay observation time. The table is currently empty, so all recorded Phase-6a cycles report no available outcome observation.

The current legacy Phase-5g experiment producer exists in v8_phase5g_context_strategy_selection_and_experiment_memory_release.py, but it relies on internal_learning_gaps as its primary source. internal_learning_gaps remains intentionally empty and productively closed.

The producer is not statically connected to the current Registry or canonical Phase-7cort cycle. If activated, it would write operationally to Phase-5g experiments, reading queue, attention and gap-related structures. It is therefore not a safe read-only bridge for the modern Shadow architecture.

The Phase-5h outcome writer exists and can evaluate Phase-5g strategy experiments, but:

The current Phase-5g and Phase-5h path is an internal strategy-projection and evaluation path, not a proven independent real-outcome path.

A read-only preflight proved that V2 Stable Observations contain all fields required for a future-only, non-productive Phase-5g Candidate contract.

No installer has been authorized. The live V2 projection population currently shows almost no strategy diversity:

The next pending read-only audit is the Modern V2 Projection Input Variance, Formula Drivers & Strategy Diversity Audit. It has been prepared but not yet executed.

During long autonomous runs, the GUI became unresponsive while the autonomous worker continued progressing. The worker was later stopped with taskkill, and SQLite quick_check and integrity_check both remained successful.

The GUI became responsive again after restarting with the same large database. Therefore:

Traditional semantic systems often focus on the what: storing and retrieving content. BrainStem focuses on the how: learning how context, uncertainty, evidence, contradiction, revision, and consolidation interact over time.

A corpus is treated as training substrate rather than as a static knowledge base. The active learning architecture forms and revises context hypotheses, preserves errors as learning material, and delays permanent knowledge promotion until consolidation and safety gates are validated.

BrainStem is an autonomous software architecture designed for continuous, self-improving data processing and knowledge management. At its core, the system operates through an Autonomous Loop that orchestrates a chain of learning phases to ingest, analyze, and refine information without manual intervention.

It is important to understand that the biological terminology used throughout the project’s technical documentation, such as "neuromodulators," "sleep," or "homeostasis" serves as a set of metaphors to describe the underlying digital functions. These biological terms are mapped to mathematical variables and algorithmic states that steer the system's behavior.

Dynamic Steering Variables: What the documentation calls "digital messenger substances" are actually dynamic meta-parameters. These variables, such as "dopamine" or "serotonin" equivalents, represent numerical values that adjust the system's learning rate, error weighting, and exploration strategies in real-time.

Active vs. Offline Processing: The system cycles between an active ingestion phase and an optimization phase. During active processing, the system extracts "context hypotheses" from new data inputs. During the optimization phase (metaphorically called "sleep"), the system re-evaluates these recorded hypotheses through batch replay and consolidation to improve overall accuracy and stability.

Knowledge Distillation: By comparing new data against existing stable records, the system filters out inconsistencies and promotes reliable information into its long-term memory structures.

Equilibrium Control: To prevent the control variables from reaching unproductive extreme values (saturation), the system uses stability monitoring routines. These routines act as a feedback mechanism that pulls meta-parameters back into a functional range when the system detects a performance plateau or excessive variance.

Adaptive Boundaries: The limits within which the system operates are not hardcoded but self-regulating. The software learns from its own performance metrics (L2M metrics) to expand or contract its processing thresholds based on the complexity of the data it encounters.

In summary, the project is a recursive learning engine that uses bio-inspired metaphors to implement a highly flexible, self-governing control logic for automated knowledge acquisition.

Runtime phases are loaded through ki_system/phase_registry.py. The registry defines load order, isolates module-loading failures, and verifies the managed-cycle top phase.

7cort Cortisol → 7g BDNF → 7f Orexin → 7e Histamine → 7d Slow-Wave → 7c E/I → 7b1 Wake-Chain Bridge → 7b Endocannabinoids → 7a Adenosine → 6d → 6c → 6b → 6a

The cleaned chain uses Phase 7b1 as orchestrator so a normal global cycle produces one complete Phase-6a replay path, one Phase-7c E/I event, one Phase-7d cycle, and one workpoint-observer event.

BrainStem currently uses 12 digital neuromodulators. Their values are normalized to [0.0, 1.0] and derived from internal system state under bounded, biologically inspired dynamics and homeostatic constraints.

GABA currently regulates system-level inhibition. It does not identify or suppress individual words, relations, or extraction errors.

All 12 displays are connected both statically and at runtime in the GUI.

Phase 6a performs offline-style replay after the wake path. Phase 6b evaluates replay effectiveness and plasticity adjustments. The critic gate checks whether proposed changes remain consistent enough to be retained; rejected or unstable material remains available as error and revision evidence.

Phase 7d adds sub-1-Hz up/down-state processing with:

A passive Phase-7d workpoint observer records longitudinal E/I state, activity, survivor ratios, reference movement, and virtual adjustment proposals without applying them.

This separation prevents Phase 6a from overwriting the active Phase-7c state on the next cycle.

Stage B will not open all write capabilities at once. The controlled sequence is:

Stage B remains blocked until the current Shadow bridge has demonstrably processed real candidates.

The following productive paths remain disabled:

The active architecture does not use word blacklists or hard-coded linguistic filters.

BrainStem uses ki_memory.sqlite3 in the project root. The database is created automatically when absent.

Learning state can be reset without re-importing the corpus. Preserved content includes documents, chunks, FTS data, import state, and configuration. The reset workflow performs a dry run and creates a timestamped database backup before applying changes.

Performance indexes are ensured during bootstrap. Bounded pruning is limited to explicitly approved history tables. Active state, Phase-5f/5g/5i data, and other protected tables are excluded from generic pruning.

Periodic autonomous execution of the approved pruning routine remains an open maintenance item.

The GUI includes a sensory-deprivation mode that skips new wake/read input while replay, consolidation, and neuromodulatory dynamics continue.

The completed 1,344-cycle no-input test supported the Stage-A stability decision.

The GUI remains an experimental testing interface; individual areas may still be incomplete.

A Windows zimdump.exe build and its required DLL files must be placed in the project root next to main.py. Users must provide their own ZIM corpus.

The current development corpus is the German Wikipedia category Computer, imported into roughly 102,000 chunks.

After major refactorings, project documentation includes a Legacy Report with:

BrainStem does not claim that every current hypothesis is meaningful or that the system understands language at a human level. The current objective is to establish and validate the mechanisms by which hypotheses are formed, challenged, revised, inhibited, replayed, and consolidated.

Current zero-result Shadow measurements do not prove a defect and do not prove successful candidate processing. Candidate-flow evidence is the next required result.

BrainStem is an experimental cognitive-architecture research project. Biological terminology is used as an engineering analogy and design inspiration. The software is not a biological simulation and does not claim neuroscientific equivalence.

title: Private mission launches to extend life of out-of-gas communication satellites
description: A two-armed space robot blasted off on a private salvage operation Tuesday to slap life-extending jetpacks on old satellites running low on fuel thousands of miles up.
fullText:
edited by 
 Andrew Zinin

A two-armed space robot blasted off on a private salvage operation Tuesday to slap life-extending jetpacks on old satellites running low on fuel thousands of miles up.

It's the second satellite-saving mission to launch this month, all part of a growing, money-saving effort to keep spacecraft running as long as possible. Another company's three-armed spacecraft rocketed into orbit on July 3 to boost NASA's Swift Observatory so it doesn't crash to Earth this fall.

Launched by SpaceX, Northrop Grumman's mission robotic vehicle—dubbed MRV—and its jetpacks will spend the next year angling into the proper orbit 22,300 miles (36,000 kilometers) above Earth. Hundreds of satellites orbit at this so-called geosynchronous orbit, where they match the speed of Earth's rotation and keep to the same part of the sky for continuous coverage.

Once in place by mid-2027, the minivan-sized spacecraft will use its 10-foot (9-meter) arms to attach a jetpack to an aging communication satellite. Then it will zip off to two more satellites in need.

For its debut flight, the spacecraft was accompanied by three electric-propelled jetpacks that peeled away separately following liftoff. Like the MRV, the jetpacks will use their own xenon gas thrusters to get to the desired orbit. Once in place, the jetpacks will wait for the robot to grab them, one at a time, and plug them into their designated satellites.

Each jetpack—the size of a washing machine—will provide the necessary oomph for an out-of-gas satellite to keep operating for several more years instead of retiring. If it works, it will be a boon for satellite operators SES of Luxembourg and Optus of Australia, saving them millions of dollars in replacement costs.

This is Northrop Grumman's latest foray into the satellite-servicing business. In 2019 and again in 2020, the company launched a pair of spacecraft that latched onto faltering communication satellites and steered them, providing extra years of life.

Northrop Grumman designed its new robotic helpers to be even more resourceful. The company envisions future versions repairing and relocating live satellites, and even latching onto dead ones for removal from highly trafficked orbits. The company is collaborating with the U.S. Naval Research Laboratory and the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency.

Katalyst Space Technologies, meanwhile, continues to test its Link satellite helper in orbit before rushing to the Swift Observatory's rescue. Flight controllers had to overcome initial communication and pointing problems with Link, beaming up software patches.

Launched in 2004, NASA's Swift telescope has been sinking faster than usual because of all the recent solar activity. A boost by Link in the coming weeks could give the nearly $400 million gamma ray observatory many more years of studying some of the biggest explosions in the universe.

NASA is paying Kataylst $30 million to raise Swift to its original 373-mile (600-kilometer) altitude. Northrop Grumman provided Link's ride to orbit, using a plane-launched Pegasus rocket.

Master's in physics with research experience. Long-time science news enthusiast. Plays key role in Science X's editorial success.

 Full profile →

title: A Ukrainian arms maker says it's got a new high-speed interceptor built to hunt Russia's jet-powered Shaheds
description: Kyiv has said this year that it needs cheap drones to counter Russia's jet-powered drones, the slowest of which already can cruise at 200 mph.
fullText:
A Ukrainian drone maker said it has made an interceptor that can reach top speeds of 230 mph.

That puts SkyFall's P1-Sun JetKiller in comfortable range to take down Russia's jet-powered Shaheds.

Russia has been increasingly using these more advanced loitering munitions to bombard Ukraine.

Ukrainian defense firm SkyFall said on Wednesday that it has developed an interceptor drone capable of taking down Russia's jet-powered Shaheds, a much faster class of attack drone that Moscow is using more regularly.

The P1-SUN JetKiller is designed with a top flight speed of 370 kmph, or 230 mph, at maximum altitudes of 9,000 meters, or roughly 30,000 feet, SkyFall said in a statement.

The drone can remain airborne for 15 minutes with a 500-gram explosive payload and can be configured with cameras for day or night operations, the company added.

Speed and maneuverability are major requirements for an interceptor hoping to catch and destroy a jet-powered Shahed.

Ukrainian interceptor drones were originally built to counter the Geran-1 and Geran-2, Russia's versions of the Iranian propeller-driven Shahed. These fly at top speeds of about 115 mph, but newer models with jet engines, called Geran-3s, can cruise at 200 mph.

Making cheap drones to counter these faster Shaheds has been an uphill task for Ukraine. As Business Insider previously reported, souping up an interceptor drone, which has to be mass-producible and often uses propellers, to reach such speeds requires copious fine-tuning.

The sheer amount of power needed to push quadcopters to 200 mph and above risks overheating the drones, which SkyFall said wouldn't be a problem with the JetKiller in straight-line flight.

SkyFall said the JetKiller is also equipped with an AI-based terminal guidance system, which essentially is a targeting algorithm to guide the drone just as it's about to hit its quarry. Such tech has increasingly been appearing in Ukrainian drones this year.

SkyFall added that the system "allows the interceptor to detect and lock onto enemy targets faster than the human eye at ranges of up to 1 kilometer."

"What's notable is that it's been used already with confirmed intercepts and is reaching mass production," said a company spokesperson, identified only by his call sign, Ares, for security purposes.

Ares told Business Insider that the JetKiller has destroyed over a dozen "jet- powered threats" on the battlefield, and that SkyFall intends to start mass production of these interceptors in August with a monthly target of 50,000 drones.

Kyiv has repeatedly said this year that it's prioritizing low-cost interceptor tech to counter Russia's jet-powered drones. Moscow fields at least three major types of attack drones with jet engines, including the Geran-5, which Ukrainian intelligence warned in January can fly at up to 373 mph.

The JetKiller is now one of the few interceptor drones that Ukrainian manufacturers say can fill the role. The Wild Hornets, another drone maker, told Business Insider's Jake Epstein in May that it's testing a new version of its Sting interceptor designed to destroy jet-powered Shaheds.

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: Arizona Senate president who gave 2020 election records to FBI wins attorney general GOP primary
description: Arizona Senate President Warren Petersen (R), who complied with a federal grand jury subpoena and gave the FBI records pertaining to the 2020 elections in March, is projected to win the Republican nom...
fullText:
Arizona Senate President Warren Petersen (R), who complied with a federal grand jury subpoena and gave the FBI records pertaining to the 2020 elections in March, is projected to win the Republican nomination to take on Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes (D) in November, according to Decision Desk HQ.

Petersen, who's served as the top Senate Republican since 2023, defeated Rodney Glassman, a lieutenant colonel in the U.S. Air Force JAG Corps Reserve and an attorney, in the GOP contest for Arizona attorney general.

The Arizona Senate president secured endorsements from Rep. Andy Biggs (R-Ariz.), running for governor, several Republican attorneys general in other states and the Arizona Fraternal Order of Police.

Petersen was involved in the Arizona Senate's independent audit of the 2020 state election results conducted by independent firm Cyber Ninjas, which confirmed former President Biden's victory in Maricopa County that election cycle— a move made as President Trump falsely claimed he won the state and others in 2020.

Mayes ahead of the primary has criticized Petersen for his claims about the 2020 election, saying Petersen "should stop lying about our elections and Republicans running for office must denounce his dangerous rhetoric."

In March, the Arizona Senate president said he turned over 2020 election records to the FBI as the Justice Department investigates unsubstantiated claims of fraud.

During a GOP debate in May, Glassman, a former Democrat-turned-Republican, suggested that officials hadn't done enough to investigate the purported fraud that took place in 2020 but did acknowledge that Biden won the election, according to KTAR.

Multiple audits and investigations have confirmed Biden's victory in 2020.

title: Lindell, Tafoya widening leads in Minnesota GOP primaries: Poll
description: A new poll shows both businessman Mike Lindell and political and cultural commentator Michele Tafoya gaining momentum in the Minnesota Republican gubernatorial and Senate primaries, respectively. The ...
fullText:
A new poll shows both businessman Mike Lindell and political and cultural commentator Michele Tafoya gaining momentum in the Minnesota Republican gubernatorial and Senate primaries, respectively.

The poll, conducted by KSTP/SurveyUSA, shows that while the GOP frontrunners are gaining bigger leads, the Democratic Senate primary between Rep. Angie Craig (Minn.) and Lt. Gov. Peggy Flanagan is still tightening.

Lindell leads the Republican gubernatorial primary by 9 points, with 35 percent of support, according to the poll. House Speaker Lisa Demuth is in second place with 26 percent, and GOP-endorsed Kendall Qualls is in third with 11 percent. Twenty-two percent of respondents were undecided. Lindell recently received a "complete and total" endorsement from President Trump, calling him "one of America's greatest and most hard working patriots" in a post on Truth Social.

A federal judge in Minnesota ruled against the "Pillow Man" last year over his claims that Smartmatic voting machines helped rig the 2020 presidential election.

In the last month, Lindell selected Phillip Parish, a retired Navy lieutenant commander, for his running mate after Parish ended his own Republican gubernatorial campaign.

Candidates want to flip the Minnesota governor seat from Tim Walz (D), who after two terms will not be seeking reelection. The former vice president hopeful came under heavy criticism for his handling of the state's fraud scandal.

Prosecutors alleged as much as $9 billion was stolen by various groups in reimbursements for meal assistance aid and other services.

The Minnesota Republican Senate Primary has Tafoya leading by around 30 points, with 41 percent of the vote. She hopes to flip retiring Democratic Sen. Tina Smith's seat.

Royce White, the former professional basketball player who previously ran for state Senate came in second place with 10 percent of support; Tom Weiler, a Navy veteran who previously ran for the House received 7 percent; Adam Schwarze, a combat veteran backed by the Minnesota GOP, received 5 percent. Twenty-eight percent of respondents were undecided.

The Minnesota primaries will be held on Aug. 11.

title: House committee urges DOJ to charge former Trump prosecutor Jack Smith
description: The GOP chair of the House Judiciary Committee encouraged the DOJ to look into criminally charging Jack Smith, alleging the former special counsel who brought charges against President Trump lied to C...
fullText:
The Republican chair of the House Judiciary Committee encouraged the Justice Department to look into criminally charging Jack Smith, alleging the former special counsel who brought charges against President Trump lied to Congress — allegations Smith's lawyers called "spurious."

The criminal referral Wednesday from Rep. Jim Jordan of Ohio stems from last year's revelation that investigators obtained several Republican lawmakers' phone records as part of a probe into Mr. Trump's actions after the 2020 election. In a deposition last year, Smith testified that his team didn't receive the contents of any phone calls or text messages since the records sought were "tolling records," which only include details like the numbers a person called.

But last week, the Justice Department told lawmakers that in a separate subpoena for records held by the National Archives and Records Administration, Smith's team obtained text messages between Trump-era White House staffers and dozens of members of Congress.

In light of that information, Jordan accused Smith of making "materially false statements" and said he improperly "failed to disclose" the subpoena to the National Archives.

"All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different," Jordan wrote. "Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee's oversight."

Jordan asked Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to investigate Smith. It is up to the Justice Department whether to pursue criminal charges following a criminal referral from Congress.

A Justice Department spokesperson confirmed to CBS News that it has received the referral letter and said it "will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct."

Mr. Trump has called for Smith's prosecution in the past. The president has frequently lashed out at officials who investigated him, including former FBI Director James Comey and New York Attorney General Letitia James, both of whom were criminally charged.

In a letter to Jordan and Blanche, attorneys for Smith called his answers to the committee's questions "unimpeachably truthful." They noted that Smith was asked about the subpoena for GOP lawmakers' tolling records, not the request for records from the National Archives. They also said Smith's team had disclosed the tech messages to Mr. Trump's lawyers, who didn't suggest at the time that anything "unlawful or improper" occurred.

"This referral reflects a chilling eagerness to weaponize the justice system by attacking a career, nonpartisan public servant who faithfully and fearlessly performed his duties by following the facts and the law," the letter read.

The House Judiciary Committee's top Democrat, Rep. Jamie Raskin of Maryland, called the criminal referral "baseless and vindictive," arguing in a statement that the case "will get laughed out of court" if it's heard by a judge.

"The Republicans fault Mr. Smith for not reading the minds of congressional investigators and for not volunteering information they didn't ask for," Raskin wrote Wednesday night. "This is absurd. Mr. Smith may be telegenic but he is not telepathic."

Biden-era Attorney General Merrick Garland named Smith to the role of special counsel in late 2022, shortly after Mr. Trump launched his 2024 presidential campaign. He was put in charge of two Justice Department probes: One focused on Mr. Trump's attempts to stay in office despite his 2020 election loss, and another looked at Mr. Trump's handling of sensitive government documents that he took to his Mar-a-Lago club after leaving the White House.

Smith ultimately brought charges in both of those cases, marking the first-ever federal prosecution of a former president — and drawing the ire of Mr. Trump, who denied wrongdoing and accused Smith of acting out of political animus.

Both of the cases against Mr. Trump ended without charges in 2024. A judge tossed out the classified documents indictment on the grounds that Smith was invalidly appointed, and the Supreme Court threw a wrench in the 2020 election indictment by ruling that Mr. Trump is entitled to broad immunity for official acts. Smith dropped the election case after Mr. Trump won the 2024 race, but argued in a final report that Mr. Trump would have been convicted otherwise.

Both Smith's final report and the 2023 indictment for election-related crimes delved into phone calls between lawmakers and Mr. Trump after the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol riot. The indictment alleged the calls were part of a last-ditch attempt by the president to "exploit the violence" and talk Republicans into blocking Joe Biden's victory from being certified.

The Senate Judiciary Committee revealed in October that the FBI analyzed the phone tolling records of eight Republican senators and one GOP House member, covering activity in the days surrounding Jan. 6, 2021.

The committee's chair, GOP Sen. Chuck Grassley of Iowa, called the discovery "disturbing and outrageous" at the time, and alleged the FBI had "spied" on members of Congress. Grassley argued it was the latest example of "weaponization" of law enforcement — a theme Mr. Trump has often echoed when describing investigations into him and his allies.

Smith has long defended his conduct. His attorneys wrote in a letter last year: "It is well established that obtaining telephone toll records pursuant to a subpoena is a routine and lawful investigative step that does not violate an individual's expectation of privacy."

Then, last week, Grassley released a letter from the Justice Department that listed out 44 lawmakers whose text messages with the White House were sent to investigators on Smith's team as part of the subpoena to the National Archives. The vast majority of the lawmakers in question were Republicans, though four Democrats are also listed, including Democratic Sen. Cory Booker of New Jersey.

The letter said the texts were almost immediately sent over to Smith's staff, preventing them from being reviewed by a "filter team," or a separate team responsible for screening evidence that might be privileged before investigators can look.

Grassley accused investigators of running "roughshod over the Constitution," arguing the texts could be protected by the Speech or Debate Clause, which significantly limits criminal prosecutions of members of Congress. Wednesday's criminal referral from Jordan also alleged Smith "likely circumvented key constitutional protections."

"Jack Smith's criminal investigation of President Trump was a runaway train that had no brakes," Grassley said last week. "I hope my Democrat colleagues, several of whom had their own texts swept up, finally put partisanship aside and recognize the severity of these actions."

Smith's attorneys pushed back in their letter Wednesday night, writing that the Speech or Debate Clause "does not prevent prosecutors from using or reviewing text messages between Members of Congress and members of the Executive Branch in an investigation and prosecution of an Executive Branch official, which is precisely what happened here."

They also said the filter team didn't need to review the White House records since that process exists primarily to sort out evidence covered by protections like attorney-client privilege.

"Mr. Smith approached the investigation into President Trump as he has approached every other investigation throughout his career as a prosecutor-following the facts and the law while adhering to Department of Justice protocol and regulations," Smith's lawyers said.

title: Cornyn approves Trump nominees, says he got assurances on foreign aid program
description: Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) on Wednesday voted to confirm two of President Trump’s nominees for diplomatic posts after briefly threatening to hold the nominations up over his concerns about frozen fund...
fullText:
Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) on Wednesday voted to confirm two of President Trump's nominees for diplomatic posts after briefly threatening to hold the nominations up over his concerns about frozen funds for a global health program.

Cornyn, in an interview with Semafor on Tuesday, said he would vote against Kari Lake and Doug Mastriano, longtime Trump allies, for two different ambassador roles if he did not see evidence that the Trump administration was releasing funds it owed for the President's Emergency Plan For Aids Relief (PEPFAR).

But during the Wednesday morning Senate Foreign Relations Committee meeting to vote on those positions, Cornyn said the administration had assured him it would release every dollar owed to PEPFAR.

A Cornyn spokesperson confirmed the senator's comments to The Hill and said "he will be closely watching future action on PEPFAR to ensure they make good on these assurances ahead of a floor vote on these nominations."

PEPFAR was established by former President George W. Bush to distribute lifesaving HIV and AIDS prevention treatment around the world. The Trump administration paused the flow of funds to the program last year before restarting it, but groups who receive that money have said delays and cuts have still occurred.

Lake's and Mastriano's nominations were advanced out of the committee by a vote of 12-10, with all Republicans for and all Democrats against. They will now be sent to the full Senate for review, where they're likely to be confirmed.

title: Over 180K cans of this popular soup recalled. See affected product
description: Over 180,000 cans of Amy’s Kitchen soup, which was sold in more than 20 states and parts of Canada, has been recalled.
fullText:
Amy's Kitchen is recalling more than 180,000 cans of soup sold in more than 20 states and parts of Canada due to a defect that could increase the risk of spoilage.

In a statement to local news station NBC Bay Area, Amy's Kitchen said the voluntary recall affects 15,530 cases of the company's "Organic Lentil Soup - Light in Sodium."

"The defect could cause the product to spoil, and the decision was made out of an abundance of caution to ensure the quality and safety of our products," the company told the local outlet.

According to a U.S. Food and Drug Administration enforcement report posted on July 14, the recall was initiated on June 22.

Amy's Kitchen did not immediately respond to USA TODAY's request for comment on Wednesday, July 22.

According to the same enforcement report from the FDA, the soup was sold in the following states:

Arizona, California, Colorado, Connecticut, Florida, Georgia, Iowa, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Maryland, Maine, Missouri, North Carolina, New Hampshire, New Jersey, Nevada, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Virginia, Washington, and Wisconsin.

The affected soups have a best-by date of April 2027 with the lot number: 60D0924.

The ongoing soup recall comes days after the FDA posted another voluntary recall affecting more than 730,000 Pillbury rolls sold to Walmart because they could contain pieces of glass.

The recall posted on July 13 said General Mills, on June 19, recalled 3,800 cases of Pillsbury Bread Rolls Hard Roll Dough and 1,260 cases of Pillsbury Bread Rolls Kaiser Roll Dough. The rolls may contain "potential foreign material (glass)," the notice reads.

The dough – sold to Walmart, which makes the rolls to sell in its bakeries – was recalled from 19 states where it was distributed. Any baked rolls were removed from bakery shelves, according to General Mills.

USA TODAY's Mike Snider contributed to this report.

Fernando Cervantes Jr. is a trending news reporter for USA TODAY. Reach him at fernando.cervantes@usatodayco.com and follow him on X @fern_cerv_.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Over 180K cans of this popular soup recalled. See affected product

title: Iran regime change talk resurfaces as Trump says Tehran 'wants to meet' amid US strikes
description: Trump administration officials are reportedly debating whether to support Iran's downfall, even as Trump says Iran is desperately seeking talks.
fullText:
Elements of the Trump administration are again debating whether the United States should support the downfall of Iran's government as the war intensifies again, according to a recent analysis.

The analysis, published Monday by Financial Times columnist Gideon Rachman, says the discussion reportedly includes efforts to encourage an internal uprising and the possible revival of proposals to support armed Iranian opposition groups, including Kurdish factions that have fought Iran for decades.

The question has gained urgency as the United States resumes strikes against Iranian military targets and officials debate whether continued pressure should remain narrowly focused on maritime security or evolve into a broader effort to weaken the Islamic Republic from within.

Such a shift would present President Donald Trump with a consequential choice: continue using military and economic pressure to force Iranian concessions, or adopt the far more ambitious objective of helping Iranians remove the Islamic Republic from power.

Iran Requests Talks With Us As White House Warns 'Violence Will Be Met With Violence'

Trump, however, signaled Tuesday that he has not closed the door on diplomacy, saying Iran is seeking talks behind the scenes.

"They want to meet desperately to try and end it because they're getting decimated," Trump told reporters during a Tuesday press conference.

Asked what talks were possible, Trump said: "They want to desperately meet. And until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest…"

"Honestly, they haven't seen anything yet. We've been nice," the president added.

Us Official Says Jd Vance Made 'Great Progress' In Iran Talks, Calls Snub Reports 'Foreign Propaganda'

Trump's comments suggest that military pressure and diplomacy remain intertwined, even as questions grow over whether some administration officials favor a broader strategy aimed at weakening or ultimately replacing Iran's leadership.

U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM), the military command responsible for American operations across the Middle East, said Monday that it struck Iranian military command centers, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and air-defense systems as part of an effort to reduce Iran's ability to threaten shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

The current military mission remains focused on protecting global shipping, Secretary of State Marco Rubio similarly said Sunday.

"The only reason why the U.S. is conducting strikes is against assets of Iran that are being used to attack global commercial shipping," Rubio told reporters Sunday at Joint Base Andrews in Maryland. "That's what the objective of this mission is. That's what it continues to be."

Experts say the distinction between weakening the regime and making its collapse a formal military objective is critical.

"Collapsing the regime remains a long-term goal using all forms of national power, but it doesn't appear to be a military objective right now," Richard Goldberg, a senior advisor at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies think tank, told Fox News Digital. "This is Project Freedom — it's focused on securing the Strait of Hormuz, and any escalation is likely tied to that mission."

Goldberg said some high-value targets, including nuclear facilities such as Pickaxe Mountain, could be treated as exceptions because of their strategic importance. Pickaxe Mountain is a deeply buried and largely uninspected complex near Iran's Natanz nuclear facility that U.S. and Israeli officials suspect could be connected to Iran's nuclear program.

But he argued that most current U.S. operations are designed to advance maritime security rather than trigger a broader campaign to topple the government.

Steve Forbes Urges Trump To Keep The Strait Of Hormuz Open At All Costs

"Where this gets confusing is when Central Command targets assets that are necessary for the Hormuz mission but could still provoke wider escalation, like dual-use infrastructure or certain leadership figures," Goldberg said. "That doesn't mean the U.S. wouldn't welcome the regime's collapse, but that outcome is more likely to come through economic pressure, covert action and targeted decapitations over time."

Any move toward regime change would immediately raise questions about who could replace Iran's rulers and whether Washington could support a political transition without becoming responsible for another prolonged Middle Eastern conflict.

One proposal reportedly discussed earlier in the war involved assisting Iranian Kurdish groups operating near the Iraqi border. Trump was open to supporting armed factions willing to fight Iran's government, U.S. officials told The Wall Street Journal in March.

The administration had contacted Kurdish leaders about possible assistance, The Washington Post separately reported.

Such groups could provide Washington with partners on the ground without the deployment of U.S. forces. But they remain divided, militarily constrained and vulnerable to retaliation against their bases and supporters in neighboring Iraq.

The central question is whether those groups are now positioned to exploit perceived weakness inside Iran or whether increased fighting amounts to another round of limited clashes.

Majeed Gly, president of the American Kurdish Committee, said recent clashes involving Kurdish groups indicate a change in how some opposition forces perceive the regime's strength. He cautioned that the violence should not be mistaken for a full-scale uprising, but said it should not be dismissed as routine border fighting.

"What I'm hearing is, this is not business as usual," Gly told Fox News Digital earlier in July. "This is not like periodic clashes on the border. This is operations, and it seems to be deep inside."

"What has changed is the perception of weakness of Iran," Gly added. "They are less afraid of the regime."

The broader Iranian opposition remains fragmented among monarchists, ethnic movements, secular activists, left-wing organizations and other groups that disagree over leadership and the structure of a future government.

That leaves the administration confronting the same problem that has complicated previous attempts to encourage political change in Iran: widespread opposition to the Islamic Republic does not necessarily translate into a unified movement capable of replacing it.

Original article

title: Poultry firm recalls nearly 1.6 million dozen eggs over potential salmonella, US FDA says
description: Food and Drug Administration said on Wednesday that ‌Midwest Poultry Services is recalling nearly 1.6 ‌million dozen white shell eggs and brown cage-free shell ​eggs produced in Texas, as they have th...
fullText:
July 23 (Reuters) - The U.S. Food and Drug Administration said on Wednesday that ‌Midwest Poultry Services is recalling nearly 1.6 ‌million dozen white shell eggs and brown cage-free shell ​eggs produced in Texas, as they have the potential to be contaminated with Salmonella Enteritidis.

The recalled eggs were available at Kroger stores ‌in Texas and ⁠Louisiana and Brookshire Grocery stores in states including Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Mississippi, ⁠the FDA said.

Midwest Poultry Services and Kroger did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for ​comment outside ​regular business hours, ​while Brookshire Grocery ‌could not be immediately reached.

No illnesses reported were linked to the Midwest Poultry Services eggs as of the announcement, the FDA added.

Midwest Poultry Services has halted distributing eggs produced on ‌its Texas farms, the ​health regulator added.

Salmonella enteritidis is ​a bacterium ​that can live on either the ‌outside or inside of ​eggs and ​cause foodborne illness. Symptoms include cramps, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, chills, fever, and possibly headache ​within 12 ‌to 72 hours after eating.

title: France Is First European Country to Implement Social Media Ban
description: On Tuesday, French lawmakers approved a ban on social media for children below age 15. France becomes the first in Europe to follow Australia, where a social media ban for kids under 16 was implemente...
fullText: On Tuesday, French lawmakers approved a ban on social media for children below age 15. France becomes the first in Europe to follow Australia, where a social media ban for kids under 16 was implemented in December. President Emmanuel Macron aims to have the law in place in time for the start of the next academic year.

title: More than 10,000 evacuated in southwestern France as wildfire spreads
description: SAUMOS, France, July 23 (Reuters) - More than 10,000 people were evacuated overnight in southwestern France as a rapidly spreading wildfire scorched through 2,000 hectares of land west of the city of
fullText:
SAUMOS, France, July 23 (Reuters) - More than 10,000 people were evacuated ‌overnight in southwestern France ‌as a rapidly spreading wildfire scorched through 2,000 ​hectares of land west of the city of Bordeaux, officials said on Thursday.

Wildfires have burnt more land ‌in Europe ⁠already this year than the annual average in the ⁠past two decades, data shows, as the world's fastest-warming continent has ​endured three ​stifling, virtually ​back-to-back heatwaves so ‌far this year.

Five hundred firefighters are battling to control the blaze, the prefecture said, adding that the wildfire was very rough, but that ‌no one has ​been injured so far.

The ​wildfire is ​located near the Arcachon ‌Bay area, a tourist ​hotspot, and ​many of those evacuated are tourists staying in camping sites.

(Reporting ​by Stephane ‌Mahé , Zhifan Liu, Dominique Vidalon; ​Writing by Ingrid Melander; Editing ​by Alexandra Hudson)

title: The borough where families are slowly disappearing
description: Islington is a snapshot of a trend playing out across inner London, Britain and much of the world.
fullText:
Islington seems, on the surface, a family-friendly area. Parents push buggies through quiet leafy side streets of this popular neighbourhood that is also within easy reach of central London.

But this north London borough has one of the lowest fertility rates in the country and mothers are older on average here than in most other parts of the capital.

Islington is a snapshot of a trend also playing out not only in inner London but also other parts of the world.

The situation has been described by the London Assembly as an "existential challenge" for the city as a whole, so what are the reasons behind this and what can be done about it?

Keen to find out more about family life in Islington, I joined a Zip-Zap baby music class to speak to parents.

Tiphanie Frutuoso and her husband had their first baby this year.

"My husband and I have a one-bedroom flat – a two-bedroom is super expensive.

"Childcare is £2,000-£3,000 a month."

They are moving back to Luxembourg, partly because childcare is free.

"It's really sad because we love London. We have better job opportunities here," Frutuoso added.

Tina Meschino had her first child at 41.

"I've always been career-driven. I moved to London from Australia for career opportunities.

"It's taken a while to get to where I want to be – an executive position – which felt like the right time to start a family."

But she added that it was very difficult to find a suitable home with enough space, within budget.

Samantha Nagaratnam had her daughter at 35.

"I was moving forward in my career and was really, really focused on that. But nobody tells you how hard it is [to fall pregnant]. It took about three years when we started trying."

Originally from Toronto, she said it was much harder to find a family home in Islington with enough space.

Zip-Zap co-founder Laura Edwards says families are moving out of Islington to other boroughs for better schools, more affordable homes and more space.

"It's going to have huge implications in 40-50 years when there's nobody to do the jobs and support the economy."

Some families have told me they have decided to stay in the borough but it is proving to be really difficult.

Father-of-two Patrick Penny-Annang has lived in Islington all his life and now shares a one-bedroom flat with his family.

"Our living room becomes our bedroom... our one bed becomes the kids' room," he said.

"Islington is a lovely area to live in but because of that it's really expensive.

"Schools have been closing, schools that I used to go to."

Between 2013 and 2023, London's population of 0–9-year-olds fell by 99,100, despite the capital's overall population growing by 506,000. The decline has been steeper in inner than outer London.

"It's an existential challenge for London," says Bassam Mahfouz, chairman of the London Assembly's Economy, Culture and Skills Committee.

His committee's recent report recommends pursuing UNICEF Child Friendly City status, introducing child-friendly housing standards, and mapping play and recreation spaces across boroughs.

"We need to tackle this as an absolute emergency.

"What's happening in Islington is happening right across London," he said, warning the trend threatens the city's workforce.

He is urging the government and the mayor to "redouble their efforts" to ensure "children are growing up in every postcode".

The fertility rate measures the number of children born per woman of childbearing age.

At 0.99, Islington has the second-lowest total fertility rate in the capital after the City of London, and one of the lowest in England.

To put it in perspective, the estimated number of children born per woman fell to just under 1.4 for England and Wales in 2025, down from 1.9 in 2010.

BBC England's data unit analysed some of the factors that may be contributing to Islington's low rate:

Young women leaving: In 2025, Islington recorded a net loss of 1,062 women aged 30-34, one of the biggest declines for that age group in the country

High rents: In 2023-24, renters spent 46% of the median local income on rent, giving Islington the ninth-highest rent-to-income ratio among 316 councils in England and Wales and only eight boroughs – all in London – ranked higher

Unaffordable housing: In 2025, the median house price was almost 12 times the median annual income, placing Islington among the 40 least affordable local authorities in England

Social housing demand: As of 31 March 2025, 16,794 households were on Islington's social housing waiting list – the 17th highest in England and sixth highest in London

Expensive childcare: Parents paid an average £9.39 an hour for two-year-olds - the 12th highest in England.

Francesca Baker-Brooker, 39, who works in PR, and her husband Andy, 41, an accountant, have decided not to have children.

The couple live in a two-bedroom flat in Islington. They both say they shared a "gut instinct" early in their relationship that parenthood was not for them.

"We like our lifestyle - we like running, going to festivals and gigs," Francesca said.

Soon after that conversation, they moved from a three-bedroom house in Kent to a two-bedroom flat in Islington and Andy had a vasectomy.

"I don't know how feasible it would have been to move to London if we'd wanted to start a family," Andy said.

When people ask if he has a family, Andy says his answer is: "I have a family – my wife."

They say about half of their friends in London do not have children, although they believe that is far less common outside the capital.

"I think it definitely is easier to be child-free in London," he added.

Katie Beck, a policy fellow at the London School of Economics' LSE Cities, said: "People want to have children in London, but there are barriers – namely the cost of living and childcare.

"People are not able to stay in London to have children, or once they do they're moving to outer boroughs or leaving London altogether."

Beck says London's falling rates of children reflects a wider global trend, driven by housing and childcare costs, more women entering higher education and the workforce, changing parenting expectations, fewer teenage pregnancies, and growing concerns about the future.

She points to boroughs such as Islington, where high poverty, limited green space, expensive housing and dense populations contribute to particularly low child populations.

I was keen to find out what the younger generation think, so spoke to a group of students.

Jess Rolf, 23, says having children was "never something she worried about", and trying to build a settled life was "very stressful with the cost of living and the job market".

"I'm from Lincolnshire and people my age back home are buying houses with their partners and talking about starting families.

"I don't think that's on the cards for me."

Charlotte Ward, 26, has said planning ahead is difficult.

"A lot of people our age are quite disenfranchised.

"You put all your energy into school, university and work, then wonder, 'do I want to bring somebody else into that if I can't even support myself right now?'

"When parents or grandparents ask 'what happens when you have children?', I can say, 'I might not'.

"There's a small element of emancipation from the expectation that you definitely will have children but if I'm progressing in my career and my male counterparts don't have to take time out to have a child, that could put me at a disadvantage."

Millicent Angel, 24, says it is hard to envisage a future when the world feels "so unstable" but she still definitely wants children.

She adds there's "a million and one things" she would like to do first, including developing her career.

Beck argues that "children are an indicator species".

"They are a sign of a healthy city. If you see a dearth of children, that rings alarm bells that a city is not functioning as it should," she added.

She describes a child-friendly city as one that is accessible, with active transport and safe affordable housing.

As the independent think tank Centre for London points out: "London is not becoming a city without families with children, but it is becoming a place where the realities that families with children face are increasingly unequal."

A spokesperson for the Mayor of London, Sir Sadiq Khan said: "The mayor wants London to remain a city for everyone – where people can afford to put down roots, raise a family and build a future.

"That is why tackling London's housing crisis and increasing the supply of genuinely affordable homes is one of his top priorities, alongside measures to support families with the cost of living, including funding free school meals for every child in a state primary school."

They added that he would "continue working with the government to build more affordable homes, support public services and ensure London remains a great place for families and children".

A government spokesperson said: "We've made paternity and unpaid parental leave day one rights through our Employment Rights Act, and have rolled out 30 hours of funded childcare a week to working families."

The government added that it was also building more council homes to back "families at every stage".

Listen to the best of BBC Radio London on Sounds and follow BBC London on Facebook, X and Instagram. Send your story ideas to hello.bbclondon@bbc.co.uk

title: Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8%
description: Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity.
fullText:
Global hunger declined for the third consecutive year, according to the Food and Agriculture Organization's (FAO) The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2026 report.

The new report estimates that 7.8 percent of the world's people were undernourished in 2025.

The FAO report notes that less than 2.5 percent of people living in the U.S., Canada, Eastern Asia, and Europe are undernourished. Therefore, its calculations chiefly encompass the extent of hunger in developing countries in Africa, Latin America, and the rest of Asia.

Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity in 12 out of 13 hunger hotspots identified by the World Food Programme and the FAO.

The drop to 7.8 percent is a huge improvement since 1970, when undernourishment afflicted nearly 35 percent of people living in developing countries.

The new estimate is down from 8.1 percent in 2024 and 8.5 percent in 2023. Overall, 645 million people were still facing hunger in 2025.

While that is good news, the new estimate remains above the pre-pandemic nadir of 7.2 percent in 2017. The FAO report projects that the level of hunger will decline by 20 percent during the rest of this decade, but that 510 million people will still be undernourished in 2030. This trend falls well short of the United Nations' sustainable development goal of zero hunger by 2030.

The post Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8% appeared first on Reason.com.

title: ‘Hero' mom dies after using last strength to save 4-year-old son from Florida river
description: Nicole Grayson, 43, jumped into the St. Johns River at Ed Stone Park to save her 4-year-old son, using all her strength to keep him above water.
fullText:
A Florida "hero" mom died after using all her strength to keep her 4-year-old son above water when he fell into the St. Johns River, allowing rescuers to pull the boy to safety, authorities and her family said.

Nicole Grayson, 43, jumped into the river after her young son fell into the water at Ed Stone Park, west of DeLand, around 4 p.m. Saturday, according to the Volusia Sheriff's Office.

Investigators said the child had been playing with an unidentified object near a wooden fence along the river's edge when he dropped it over the barrier. The boy fell into the river while attempting to retrieve it.

Off-duty Ice Officer Relies On 'Sixth Sense' To Save Drowning Child Before Delivering Message To Critics

Grayson immediately went in after him.

"My guess is he was trying to get it. He slipped in the water, deep, he slipped away in the water," Grayson's oldest son, Dafino Devega, told Fox 35. "And then my mom, I guess she panicked, and she went in the water and tried to save him."

Crocodile Attack Horror Unfolds As Couple Tries To Save Victim: 'We Felt So Helpless'

As Grayson struggled to keep her son above the surface, a witness at the park flagged down a passing boater, who threw two life preservers toward the mother and child, the sheriff's office said.

Grayson eventually became submerged while continuing to hold her son above the water.

A frantic 911 caller told dispatchers that rescuers had managed to reach the child but were still struggling to recover his mother.

Firefighters, Police Pull Young Woman From Sewage-filled Ravine After Days Trapped In 104-Degree Heat

"Hey 911, can you come to Ed Stone Park?" the caller said in audio obtained by FOX 35. "We have a mother that's drowned, and we got the baby, but we can't get the mama."

The boater maneuvered close enough to pull the 4-year-old aboard. The boy was unharmed, according to authorities.

Witness Jon Reaka said Grayson appeared to expend all her energy ensuring her son survived.

"She spent all her energy trying to get her kid up over the edge," Reaka told FOX 35.

Reaka jumped over a railing and onto the boat, where he helped pull Grayson from the river. She was unresponsive when she was brought aboard.

"I jumped over the rail, jumped onto the boat and pulled the lady up onto the boat and, you know, we started CPR — I started CPR and mouth-to-mouth immediately," Reaka said.

Emergency medical personnel took over lifesaving measures after arriving at the park. Grayson was rushed to a hospital, where efforts to revive her continued for several hours.

She died in the intensive care unit around 10 p.m. Saturday, the sheriff's office said.

Like What You're Reading? Click Here For More

"I tried everything I could do to save her," Reaka told FOX 35. "You know, I feel horrible. I mean, it's so sad what happened."

Grayson was a mother of six whose final act reflected the fiercely protective way she had lived, her family said.

"That's the type of mom that she was; she was a hero," Devega said.

"My mom, she was a strong woman," he added. "She would do anything to keep her kids safe."

Volusia County Sheriff Mike Chitwood praised the witnesses and the boater who rushed to help Grayson and her son.

"Sheriff Chitwood and the entire Volusia Sheriff's Office send sincere thanks all who jumped in to help with the rescue, and deepest condolences to Ms. Grayson's family and friends for their loss of a mother who gave her life to save her son," the department said in a statement.

Chitwood said Grayson would be remembered as a hero, "not just because of her final heroic act, but because she was a mom who would do anything for her kids."

Original article source: 'Hero' mom dies after using last strength to save 4-year-old son from Florida river

title: Mayor who survived assassination attempt shot dead in town hall
description: Mayor Valentin Lavin, who was allied to President Claudia Sheinbaum's leftist party, had already survived an armed attack in January, according to local media reports.
fullText:
A local mayor in central Mexico was shot dead in a town hall, authorities reported Wednesday, after the politician had survived a previous assassination attempt.

The town of Temoac is located in the state of Morelos, a region close to Mexico City where several drug cartels are active.

Mayor Valentin Lavin, who was allied to President Claudia Sheinbaum's leftist party, had already survived an armed attack in January, according to local media reports.

Forensic experts and police officers located his dead body inside local government facilities, the state prosecutor's office said.

An operation to arrest those responsible for the crime was underway, it added in a statement.

Almost 100 mayors have been murdered in Mexico since 2006, when drug-related violence surged, official figures show. Since the current government took office in October 2024, 14 mayors have been murdered in Mexico, three in 2026, Uno TV reported this week.

The country was particularly shocked by the fatal shooting of Mayor Carlos Manzo in the state of Michoacan last November, during Day of the Dead celebrations.

Criminal gangs often conduct attacks against local officials in Mexico, especially in areas where cartels fight for territory.

Last week, attackers shot dead the husband of a Mexican councilwoman, with the gunfire also injuring the politician and her daughter as the family was reportedly on their way to a school graduation, authorities said.

Sheinbaum said July 14 that homicides in Mexico have fallen by nearly half since September 2024, touting the reduction as a result of her security policies since taking office.

Mexico has faced years of escalating violence since the government launched an offensive against drug cartels in 2006. Nearly 500,000 people have been killed and around 130,000 have disappeared since then, government data shows.

title: Teachers who once dated and share child arrested after classroom argument spiraled into violence: police
description: Both teachers at The SEED School of Miami face domestic violence battery charges after an alleged assault and pepper spray attack on school property.
fullText:
A personal dispute between two teachers who were formerly a couple erupted inside a Miami charter boarding school, ending with both under arrest after investigators say a classroom meeting turned violent.

According to a Miami-Dade Schools Police arrest affidavit obtained by Fox News Digital, Terrod Tiyan Torrence, 36, and Odiria Biggins were previously involved in a romantic relationship and share a child together.

A few days before the confrontation, Biggins told investigators Torrence left a handwritten note on her desk asking if they could talk.

She agreed to meet him July 17 inside Classroom 119 at The SEED School of Miami, the affidavit said.

Sjsu Volleyball Coach's Alleged Assault Victim Speaks Out For The First Time Since Allegations Surfaced

What began as a conversation quickly turned physical, according to investigators.

Biggins told police Torrence repeatedly punched her in the left side of the head before slamming her head into a wall, causing her to lose consciousness and collapse to the floor.

Marcellus Wiley's Wife Claims Ex-nfl Star Threatened To Kill Her Before Fourth Of July Domestic Battery Arrest

But the confrontation didn't end there, investigators said.

After regaining consciousness, Biggins allegedly walked to her vehicle, grabbed a canister of pepper spray and later tracked Torrence down elsewhere on campus before spraying him, according to Miami ABC affiliate WSVN.

Surveillance video captured Biggins entering a classroom where Torrence was, the outlet reported.

Bring The Bear Spray To The Wedding — You Never Know When You Need To Use It On A Family Member

Moments later, both emerged with Torrence appearing to struggle walking and suffering visible eye irritation before school staff rushed to help him. He was later taken to a hospital for treatment.

Biggins was arrested Monday and charged with discharging a weapon on school property for allegedly using pepper spray on campus, along with misdemeanor domestic violence battery, according to WSVN. A judge found probable cause and set her bond at $2,000.

Torrence was arrested Tuesday by Miami-Dade Schools Police and charged with misdemeanor domestic violence battery.

Court records show he was booked into the Turner Guilford Knight Correctional Center before appearing before Judge Alicia Garcia Priovolos, who imposed a $2,000 standard bond and a pretrial no-contact order barring him from contacting Biggins. His arraignment is scheduled for Sept. 23.

It remains unclear what sparked the argument or whether any students witnessed the confrontation.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Miami-Dade Schools Police Department and The SEED School of Miami for additional information, including whether either teacher remains employed.

Original article

title: Bryan Johnson says he cloned himself as a newborn to grow organs
description: Johnson said for now, "baby Bryan" still lives in a Petri dish.
fullText:
(NewsNation) — Entrepreneur and "longevity guru" Bryan Johnson has taken a different approach to how he will manage his autoimmune disease: cloning himself.

"I just cloned myself … as a newborn," Johnson, 48, wrote to his followers on X. He added that for now, "baby Bryan" currently lives in a Petri dish.

In early July, Johnson announced he'd been diagnosed with autoimmune gastritis, an incurable condition in which the immune system attacks the stomach's acid-producing cells. According to the National Institutes of Health, it can cause iron and vitamin B12 deficiencies, leading to anemia.

He attributed his diagnosis to poor eating and being stressed as a father of three.

"With this clone, I can become my own blood boy, test therapies on the clone, grow organs for transplantation, develop new treatments and inject young cells," Johnson wrote.

He said the process involved doing a blood draw, extracting his cells, exposing them to Yamanaka factors and resetting them to an "embryonic-like state."

Johnson's plan quickly drew criticism from many online, with some calling it unsettling, while others said the process was just "evil." Another person wrote: "Bryan Johnson is what happens when you give too much money to a man who is morbidly afraid of death."

Others were more receptive to the plan, however, and said some medical breakthroughs in the past have been achieved through unconventional means.

The description of Johnson's cloning experiment doesn't seem to be like an actual clone. He may be describing induced pluripotent stem cells, or iPSCs. Those are mature, specialized cells (like skin or blood cells) that have been genetically reprogrammed in a lab to act like embryonic stem cells.

"I mentioned getting diagnosed with an incurable disease is one of the best things that has happened to me in a long time," Johnson continued. "It's opened up a frontier of new paths to repair and strengthen the body. This is the first example."

title: Colorado Republican unveils bill banning relationships between members and staff without Ethics disclosure
description: Rep. Jeff Crank (R-Colo.) on Wednesday introduced a resolution requiring House lawmakers to disclose relationships with staffers of other offices or committees to the lower chamber’s Committee on Ethi...
fullText:
Rep. Jeff Crank (R-Colo.) on Wednesday introduced a resolution requiring House lawmakers to disclose relationships with staffers of other offices or committees to the lower chamber's Committee on Ethics.

Crank's resolution proposes upholding the existing ban on lawmakers from dating their staffers or staffers of a committee they serve on. It also proposes mandating lawmakers confidentially disclose relationships with staffers in other offices or committees to the Ethics panel.

If the circumstances of the relationship changes, members must then give "prompt notification" to the committee.

Under the House Code of Official Conduct, members cannot engage in a "sexual relationship" with their staffers or staffers of committees they work on, with an exception for marriage.

While lawmakers cannot engage in "unwelcome sexual advances or conduct" to House employees, the code of conduct does not explicitly bar relationships between members and staffers of other offices and committees.

In a release, Crank said members of Congress "should be held to the highest standard."

The Colorado Republican added, "We must recognize that as elected officials, we can have unbalanced influence over House employees, regardless of who they work for. The personal conduct of some of my colleagues has undermined public trust in this institution, and the legislative process, and we must do better."

Crank proposed the resolution a day after Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) backed banning members from having relations with any Capitol Hill staffers.

"I can't believe it would be acceptable in any situation," Johnson said at a press conference. "You have a role where you have members of Congress and their subordinates and staff members — I think it's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace, and most companies have that."

"We should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America," Johnson said. "So, that's an easy one for me."

Last week, the New York Post reported that Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.), during his time in the House, had consensual relationships while he was not married with two aides to Texas Democrats. The Arizona Democrat told reporters last week the story was "gossip."

The Senate Ethics Committee last month dismissed a complaint against Gallego by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.), who accused the Arizona senator of making inappropriate advances toward several women and violating campaign finance rules.

Former Reps. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) and Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) also resigned earlier this year amid allegations of improper relationships with staffers — one former staffer of Swalwell's accused him of rape. Swalwell has denied wrongdoing, while Gonzales admitted to the affair with a staffer who later committed suicide.

Responding to the New York Post story about Gallego, House Republican Conference Chair Lisa McClain (R-Mich.) said Congress should bar members from dating all staffers.

"Even if a relationship is consensual, there is an inherent power imbalance that can undermine trust and create an appearance of impropriety," the No. 4 House Republican wrote on the social platform X. "The goal should be protecting staff and maintaining confidence in the institution."
