Team Wales go for Commonwealth Games gold in Glasgow
description: The 23rd Commonwealth Games are about to begin in Glasgow with Team Wales hoping for success.
fullText:
Ten sports. More than 10 days of action. 114 athletes.

The 23rd Commonwealth Games, held in Glasgow, are about to begin with Team Wales hoping for success.

Thursday's opening ceremony, held at the Hydro Arena, will signify the official start of the Games.

Day one of competition is on Friday, 24 July with a 'day zero' added prior to the opening ceremony, in order to cope with "an unprecedented demand" in bowls and para bowls.

Weeks, months and years of preparation will all culminate in athletes representing their country against 73 other countries and territories.

This is a scaled back Games after Glasgow stepped in when the Victoria bid in Australia pulled out of hosting.

Welsh athletes will compete in 3x3 wheelchair basketball, artistic gymnastics, athletics and para athletics, bowls and para bowls, boxing, judo, netball, swimming and para swimming, track cycling and para track cycling and weightlifting.

But where will Wales' medals likely come from? Who are the stars of the future who could announce themselves this summer? And when was Wales' most successful Games?

BBC Sport Wales has picked out some athletes to look out for and considers what success could looks like.

Team Wales named for Glasgow Commonwealth Games

Can Wales find new Commonwealth legends in Glasgow?

Wales will be hoping for success from a few of their stand out athletes who have already won major medals on the global stage.

Cyclist Emma Finucane collected two bronze medals in Birmingham four years ago but has since won 16 medals at the Olympics, World and European Championships.

The Paris 2024 gold medallist will compete in the keirin, sprint and team sprint when the track cycling begins on Thursday, 30 July.

Swimmer Matt Richards will be hoping to claim the one major medal that has eluded him so far in his successful career.

Four fourth place finishes in 2022 is ample fuel for his desire to complete the set in Glasgow.

At just 19, gymnast Ruby Evans has competed at an Olympics and has a silver medal at the World Championships and a British all-around title to her name.

Evans could help lead Wales to a medal in the team event as well as individual success in the all-around and floor exercise.

Wales' team captain Olivia Breen is one of a number of para athletes who could win a medal in Glasgow.

She won gold in T38 long jump in 2018 and T38 100m in 2022.

Boxer Rosie Eccles will also defend her title, in the women's light-middleweight category, in what she says will be her final Games after she was given the honour of being the flag bearer at Thursday's opening ceremony.

A name you will likely hear a lot if you are following Wales at this year's Games is Theodora Taylor.

The 17-year-old swimmer from Torfaen Dolphins could compete in multiple events in Glasgow, including relays.

Already competing against seniors, she won silver at the GB Swimming Championships in April in the 50m freestyle which led to her selection to represent Great Britain at the European Aquatics Championships in Paris next month.

Lola Hodson, 20, is one of Wales' most exciting judo prospects. She won both junior and senior titles at the British Championships in Nottingham in December and placed fifth at the Junior European Championships last year.

Former world number one and Commonwealth gold medallist Natalie Powell is now head of performance at Welsh Judo and says Hodson is, "the most talented junior we've had come through the Welsh system."

"She's way more talented than I was, her judo is way better than I was at junior level," said Powell.

"She's a great prospect, she's still a junior, it's obviously a senior competition.

"So not putting too much pressure on her, but she's a great talent and got lots of potential."

Teenage track cyclist Ioan Hepburn is thought to be a future star in the sport.

After losing a kidney when crashing his bike in a downhill race aged 13, he was initially reluctant to make the move from mountain biking to the track.

The 18-year-old was talent-spotted at an agricultural show in Usk by Beicio Cymru's sprint coach Rachel Draper when he set the highest score of the day on a Watt bike - a stationary exercise bike which measure a cyclist's raw power.

Hepburn was eventually convinced to come down to the Velodrome in Newport and has since gone on to win a Junior World Championship title aged 17.

Wales have competed at every single Commonwealth Games since the inaugural Games in Hamilton, Canada in 1930.

Their most successful Games were on the Gold Coast in 2018 where they brought home 36 medals, including 10 golds, 12 silvers and 14 bronze.

The total medals matched the highest Games haul in Glasgow in 2014, when Wales only won five golds.

The haul of 10 golds emulated the previous best in Auckland in 1990 but in those Games in New Zealand, Wales only returned with 25 medals.

On paper Wales' least successful Games were in 1950 when John Brockway's silver in the 110 yard backstroke was their only medal won in Auckland.

But given Brockway was one of only three athletes sent to the Games that year, meaning one in three athletes returned with a medal, there is an argument it was one of their most successful too.

Given the Games on this occasion has been stripped back somewhat, comparisons with previous events would be unfair.

So there will be no medal target.

"We don't talk about medals but I'd be stood here lying to you if I said it wouldn't be incredible," said Team Wales chef de mission Gethin Jones.

"I've got some flags in my bags, just in case that happens, so I can whip them out.

"What I can say is every single one of the athletes are here to represent Wales with pride and passion. I know they'll leave absolutely everything out there."

Jones says it is about giving Team Wales the chance to perform at their best.

"A lot of people talk about success," said Jones.

"Within the teams, they have their own conversations.

"As Team Wales, it's about giving them the opportunity to succeed and success is being able to deliver your best performance on the day when it matters, under pressure, with the whole world watching

"If they do that, I don't think you can ask for much more.

"If you get a personal best, that is success for me, it is about making sure they're given the environment to be able to do that.

"We'll be proud of them whatever, because we know the sacrifices, work and dedication they've put in. So I know they're raring to go."

Wales chose a high-profile chef de mission in the television presenter but it is not an ambassadorial role with Jones helping Team Wales creating a little bit of home in a hotel in Scotland.

"After all the preparation over the past 18 months and more, it's fantastic for the team to be here," said Jones.

"We've hit the ground running. It feels like we've been here for a month trying to get everything ready.

"There's much work to do to make sure all the logistics, preparation and rooms are ready for when the athletes arrive."

title: Glasgow set to welcome the world for scaled back Commonwealth Games
description: Athletes from 74 countries and territories will compete for 215 gold medals over 10 days of competition.
fullText:
When the fastest man on the planet won Commonwealth relay gold for Jamaica in 2014, more than 50,000 fans cheered him home at Hampden.

But the athletes seeking to emulate Usain Bolt when the Games return to Glasgow - including mile world record holder Josh Kerr - will be competing at Scotstoun Stadium in front of 11,000 spectators.

This time round almost everything is on a smaller scale but organisers are confident fans will create a Tartan Army-style atmosphere for the 3,000 competitors who will be chasing glory.

From Friday until 2 August, 215 gold medals will be up for grabs - compared to 261 when Scotland last staged the Games - but Glasgow 2026 will see a record 47 para-sports events.

The action gets under way with bowls and para-bowls heats at 09:30, but the Games will be officially opened by King Charles during a ceremony at the Hydro later.

It will be attended by athletes from 74 nations and territories as well as dignitaries including Prime Minister Andy Burnham, Scotland's First Minister John Swinney and the First Minister of Wales Rhun ap Iorwerth.

Some of the 4,000 Games volunteers, aged 16 to 96, will also take part in the celebration.

Glasgow stepped in when the Australian state of Victoria pulled out of hosting the Games in July 2023.

It had only agreed to stage the event in April 2022 after the Commonwealth Games Federation (CGF) struggled to find a host.

Organisers had originally estimated the event would cost A$2.6bn (£1.35bn) and the state government had billed it as a boost for the regions.

But the projected bill soared to more than A$6 billion (£3.11bn).

Victorian Premier Daniel Andrews said the new figure was "more than twice the estimated economic benefit" it would bring to the state.

The decision not only plunged the future of the 2026 edition into doubt but the Games as whole.

But in September 2024 a deal, backed by the Scottish government, was agreed for Glasgow to host "a scaled back, but high-quality" event.

It will be staged across four venues along an eight-mile stretch - Scotstoun Stadium, Tollcross International Swimming Centre, Glasgow International Arena and the Scottish Event Campus (SEC).

In 2014 the budget for the Games was £543m but no public money has been spent on the 23rd edition, which will cost £150m in total.

The CGF is investing £100m of this, with the remainder coming from commercial revenue streams such as ticketing, sponsorship, broadcast rights and merchandise.

Athletics, cycling and swimming have proved the most popular but tickets are still available for many events.

The triumphant 2012 London Olympics were still fresh in the mind when Glasgow last staged the Commonwealth Games.

Usain Bolt topped an athletics cast list which also included Olympic champions David Rudisha of Kenya (800m), Australia's Sally Pearson (100m hurdles) and Valerie Adams of New Zealand (shot put).

This year the most decorated track star is also a Jamaican sprinter.

Elaine Thompson-Herah made her Commonwealth debut in 2014 and went on to become the first woman in history to win back-to-back Olympic 100m and 200m titles in Rio and Tokyo.

She is aiming to defend the titles she won at Birmingham 2022.

Other big names include Canadian sprinter Andre De Grasse, who is a double Olympic champion, and two-time world 200m champion Shericka Jackson of Jamaica.

Meanwhile, Scotland's Eilish McColgan will be hoping to retain her Commonwealth title in the 10,000m.

Her triumph four years ago followed her mother Liz's victory in the same event at the 1986 Games in Edinburgh.

McColgan will line-up alongside compatriot Megan Keith, who broke the European 10k road record in Spain earlier this year.

Meanwhile, reigning 1500m Commonwealth champion Laura Muir will run the 5,000m.

In the pool, South Africa's Chad Le Clos was one of the biggest draws in 2014 after he defeated his hero, American legend Michael Phelps, in the 200m butterfly at the London Olympics.

Le Clos will return to Glasgow, where he won two golds in 2014, with the aim of becoming the most decorated male in the history of the Games by adding to his current haul of 18 medals.

Is Glasgow ready for the Commonwealth Games - and does the city care?

Hoy backs Glasgow 2026 for 'massive success'

Fellow Olympic champions competing at Tollcross include Mollie O'Callaghan of Australia, England's Adam Ramsay-Peaty and Daniel Wiffen of Northern Ireland.

Duncan Scott, Scotland's most successful Olympian, will hope to add to his collection of 13 Commonwealth medals.

But Archie Goodburn, who has terminal brain cancer, could provide one of the moments of the Games if he lands a podium finish in the 50m breaststroke.

Last month he told BBC Scotland: "I get goosebumps thinking about it."

Elsewhere, two five-time Commonwealth champions will be hoping to delight the home crowd.

Bowls great Alex "Tattie" Marshall will be making his eighth Games appearance and para-cycling star Neil Fachie will retire after riding both men's tandem events.

The mile returns to the Games for the first time since the 1966 edition in Kingston, Jamaica.

The race on 1 August - which will replace the 1500m - will see a stellar line-up go head-to-head over the distance made famous by Sir Roger Bannister.

In May 1954 he became the first person to run a mile in under four minutes and later that summer won gold over the same distance at the 1954 Games.

Fast forward 72 years and the line-up will include three Scots with high hopes of a medal.

Josh Kerr, fresh from breaking the 27-year-old mile world record in London, will be a favourite to top the podium.

But the former 1500m world champion will face competition from 2022 world champion Jake Wightman and Neil Gourley, who has world and European indoor 1500m medals to his name.

Australian track sensation Cameron Myers - who ran the fastest 1500m by an under-21, external in July - will also compete in the event.

In the women's mile race Scotland will be represented by Jemma Reekie.

The decision to scale back the Games meant there was some disappointment when the final schedule was announced.

Hockey was dropped despite a £5m national hockey centre being built for Glasgow 2014.

The cycling programme, which previously included mountain biking and road races, will be confined to the track.

In gymnastics, there is no room for any rhythmic events, with only artistic gymnastics being included.

Bowls, which were last time held at Kelvingrove, will be staged indoors at the SEC.

Badminton and squash were also scrapped and there will be no diving competitions.

In 2014 the baton relay visited 70 Commonwealth nations and territories over 248 days, covering a distance of 190,000km (118,060 miles).

The epic journey concluded with a 40-day trip across Scotland involving 4,000 baton bearers.

They included actor Brian Cox, Scotland manager Gordon Strachan, singer Susan Boyle, broadcaster Kirsty Wark and cycling legend Sir Chris Hoy.

This time round each competing team was given its own baton to decorate rather than transporting one across the Commonwealth.

The Scottish leg of the relay was launched on 1 July when swimmer Duncan Scott sailed up the River Clyde on a power boat.

Since then the baton has travelled to communities, events, and festivals across the country's 32 local authorities.

Celtic Park staged a spectacular opening ceremony in 2014 which featured Rod Stewart, dancing Tunnock's Tea Cakes and a Red Arrows fly over.

This year the event will be staged at the Hydro and will feature performances by headliner KT Tunstall, Callum Beattie, Nathan Evans, Tom Walker and Saint Phnx.

Inspiration for the opening ceremony has come from a variety of sources, including Bad Bunny's half-time show at this year's Superbowl.

Kylie Minogue, Lulu and Deacon Blue topped the bill for the last closing ceremony, which was staged at Hampden.

The curtain will come down on Glasgow 2026 at the Hydro after a ceremony featuring Delta Goodrem and rock legends Simple Minds.

Shankar Mahadevan, one of Bollywood's most renowned vocalists, and film star Rashmika Mandanna will also take to the stage to refelect the fact that the Indian city of Ahmedabad will host the centenary Games in October 2030.

Judo champion Sarah Adlington and Alex Marshall, who has won five Commonwealth gold medals in bowls, will lead out Team Scotland at the opening ceremony.

Adlington, one of Britain's most successful heavyweight judoka athletes, will bear the Scottish baton while Marshall will carry the Saltire flag.

Marshall said: "It will be an absolute privilege to lead this team out with Sarah, and I know every single one of us will give everything to bring success home for Team Scotland."

Adlington said: "Representing Scotland has always meant so much to me, so to have the opportunity to lead this team into a home Commonwealth Games is a real privilege that I'll cherish for the rest of my life."

The BBC was replaced by TNT Sports as live UK broadcast partner for the Games.

The subscription channel outbid the corporation, which had been the main partner and provided free-to-air coverage since 1954 for 18 games in-a-row.

Earlier this month it was confirmed BBC Alba is to screen nightly live coverage and highlights throughout the Games.

The Gaelic broadcaster will be the only place on the BBC where viewers can watch the action.

A similar deal was struck with S4C to provide live Welsh-language coverage.

Meanwhile, Channel 5 will screen the opening ceremony live and also run a nightly highlights show.

BBC Radio 5 Live is also providing extensive coverage of the Games featuring live event updates and daily evening broadcasts.

A cartoon thistle mascot was one of the stars of Glasgow 2014.

Fibreglass statues of Clyde - who was designed by 12-year-old Beth Gilmour - were dotted around the city and his beaming smile was all over social media.

Last July a unicorn called Finnie was unveiled as the official mascot for the 2026 Games.

But, earlier this month, Clyde staged a unlikely comeback as he joined Team Scotland.

Elinor Middlemiss, the team's chef de mission, said: "Clyde isn't just a mascot, he's part of Scotland's sporting story."

Five big names to look out for at Glasgow 2026

All you need to know about Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games

title: HNK Rijeka tie 'a great opportunity' for Derry
description: Derry City manager Tiernan Lynch says Thursday's Uefa Conference League second qualifying round first leg against HNK Rijeka is going to be a "great experience" for his side (19:45 BST).
fullText:
Derry City manager Tiernan Lynch says Thursday's Uefa Conference League second qualifying round first leg against HNK Rijeka is going to be a "great experience" for his side.

The Candystripes return to European action (19:45 BST) aiming to bounce back after defeat against CSKA Sofia ended their Europa League qualifying hopes.

Croatian outfit Rijeka finished fourth in their domestic league last season and reached the Conference League league phase last year.

"They have a couple of big players in there that probably make them tick. They have a player that just came back from the World Cup with the Croatian national team (Toni Fruk)," Lynch told BBC Sport NI.

"They're a very good side, a very technical side and they're very possession based. They want to get on the ball, they want to play and they want to keep the ball. All the things that you probably expect from those European teams.

"We always have to look at these as a great experience and what we can learn from them. You always want to test yourselves against better players and better teams. I think this is going to be a great opportunity for us."

Lynch's side entered the second round of Conference League qualifiers after losing 5-3 on aggregate to CSKA Sofia in the Europa League first qualifying round.

The second leg of the Europa League qualifier at the Brandywell was marred by trouble between rival supporters in the stands with the match temporarily stopped and police deployed.

Despite the defeat and the disturbances, Lynch added that his side can look back and use the match to build ahead of their opening Conference League qualifying game.

"The atmosphere last week was utterly electric. It's the best night I've had in the Brandywell. We lost the game, but I thought the fans were phenomenal," the 50-year-old continued.

"They were squeezed in there, they were loud, they kept going. They stayed with us, they pulled us over the line at times.

"I think if we can get back in the same place, ideally you would even take a draw off to be told and get yourself back on a level playing field to go into the second tie. That would be ideal for us."

As a result of the crowd trouble, Derry have been given a suspended partial stadium closure for a probationary period of two years.

They have also been fined 20,000 euro (£17,000) with CSKA Sofia handed a ban on bringing away fans to their next European fixture and a further one-game sanction suspended for two years.

"I think it's a game that probably should be remembered for football reasons," added Lynch, who was speaking before the punishment confirmed on Tuesday.

"Unfortunately, it will probably be remembered for the crowd trouble and the disturbances and the CSKA, Sofia supporters and ultras or whatever they're called.

"That's unfortunate. It wasn't nice to see as everybody has now seen the families. How intimidating it was, it was scary. My own family were in the crowd so I know how people felt."

Children flee onto pitch amid disorder at football stadium

Derry City given suspended partial stadium closure

title: Crystal Palace sports centre set for £130m revamp
description: Bromley Council is to meet to discuss plans for a major revamp of the world famous sport centre.
fullText:
Plans to transform and regenerate the world-leading Crystal Palace National Sports Centre are set to be approved by Bromley Council.

The scheme include a revamp of the athletics stadium, indoor arena, and swimming and diving facilities.

The project - spearheaded by the Greater London Authority (GLA) and London's mayor Sir Sadiq Khan - is to be discussed by Bromley Council on Thursday, where planning officers have recommended it for approval.

Supporters have praised the sporting opportunities the new centre will bring to south London, while objectors are concerned certain sports could be disadvantaged under the new plans.

Bromley planners said the proposal would "significantly enhance the quality of the existing sports provision and improve the experience of users across both the borough and London".

The planning application has received hundreds of responses from Bromley residents; 390 people wrote in to support the project, while the proposal also garnered 406 objections.

The battle to bring London Diamond League 'home' to Crystal Palace

Supporters have welcomed the chance to bring the dilapidated complex back to life, with one describing it as "the beating heart of Crystal Palace, much loved and used".

Others felt the centre's revival would reduce crime and anti-social behaviour in the area by increasing sporting opportunities.

Several sporting bodies have also thrown their support behind the project, according to the Local Democracy Reporting Service (LDRS).

England Athletics said the project would "secure the long-term future of the site and allow it to serve as a modern, inclusive, and sustainable centre for athletics".

Volleyball England "strongly supports" the regeneration, it said.

Divers have said are keen to see the diving pools brought back into use and and water polo and underwater hockey players are hopeful the 50m pool could host matches and tournaments.

Sport England said there was "overwhelming support" for the plans.

A spokesperson said: "The Crystal Palace National Sports Centre is tired and dated, and in desperate need of investment and modernisation in order to make it inclusive and accessible to all sections of the local community and to bring it into the 21st century, as well as to set in sound financial footing for the future."

However, England Hockey and the Football Foundation have objected to the plans as they think the proposed shared multi-sport artificial grass pitch would not be sufficient for the demand that both hockey and football have.

The organisations are also concerned about the loss of an artificial pitch at the centre.

The indoor climbing community has voiced several concerns about the provision for a climbing wall within the new sports centre - which is to be moved to the current 5-a-side building.

Bromley planning officers argued that the space set aside for the climbing facility was nearly three times the size of the current one.

The proposal would mean some parts of the motorsport track are built on, something objectors say "prevents its use in the future and prevents people walking the historic track".

Bromley planning officers said the entirety of the racing circuit would be retained and remain "clearly recognisable" despite the changes.

Only some sections of Stadium Straight would be narrowed to enable the inclusion of additional outdoor sporting and recreational facilities, such as 5-a-side football and 3v3 basketball.

Listen to the best of BBC Radio London on Sounds and follow BBC London on Facebook, external, X, external and Instagram, external. Send your story ideas to hello.bbclondon@bbc.co.uk, external

Sports centre revamp reaches 'key milestone'

Crystal Palace Park dinosaurs saved in restoration

title: One month, two record transfers - why English midfielders are in demand
description: Elliot Anderson and Morgan Rogers both sold for record transfer fees, so why are English midfielders so in demand?
fullText:
In the space of one month, England duo Elliot Anderson and Morgan Rogers have each broken the record for being the most expensive British player of all-time.

Anderson's £116m move from Nottingham Forest to Manchester City came before Chelsea signed Rogers for £117m from Aston Villa.

Both are English midfielders, both are operating at a high level. Both are just 23 - in theory, years from their peak.

So why are England so strong in this area? Why have the pair been sold for such high fees?

And with Adam Wharton and Alex Scott among those also attracting attention, what is it about English midfielders that makes them so sought after?

At such a relatively young age, both Rogers and Anderson offer the prospect of potential as well as being ready-made Premier League players.

Anderson arrives in Manchester having enjoyed a remarkable domestic campaign and impressed on the world stage, starting all but one of England's eight matches as they finished third at the World Cup.

The central midfielder was a real engine for the Three Lions and proved City right to spend big to bring him in.

For Forest last season, he was a bright spark in a tough campaign, spent mostly fighting relegation.

He recorded 3,300 touches in the top flight last season, more than any other player, and led the division for duels won (297), possessions won (306) and fouls won (80).

He also topped the charts for most passes completed as a central midfielder (2,038) and led the position with 376 line-breaking passes.

His four goals and four assists along with creating 54 chances, including nine big chances, highlight his proficiency at both ends of the pitch.

Meanwhile, Rogers contributed 14 goals and 12 assists in 55 appearances across all competitions for Aston Villa last season.

A versatile attacking midfielder, Rogers can play as a number 10 or out on the left wing, meaning his defensive stats do not compare to those of Anderson, but going forward he showed himself as one of the most effective players on the continent.

Across Europe's top five leagues, he ranked second-highest among midfielders for touches in the opposition box (181) while his 16 Premier League goal involvements place him inside Europe's top 10 in the position.

Statistics from the last Premier League season do not feature Manchester City maestro Rodri as he continued his return from a anterior cruciate ligament injury.

But it is likely he will return to the top of a lot of those charts next season after looking back to his best at the World Cup, winning the player of the tournament award after guiding Spain to the title.

With traditional number nines seemingly becoming a dying breed, clubs are turning to other positions to provide the goal threat.

That makes the midfielder, particularly one with a bit of creativity who can unlock defences, a sought-after player.

England seem to have produced a number of players who fit that bill within a similar time period - although Tottenham have spent £185m on a midfield duo Sandro Tonali of Italy and Portugal's Matheus Fernandes this summer.

With the Premier League's home grown rule in force - requiring a minimum of eight places in a club's 25-strong squad are set aside for players produced in England or Wales - it is also expected that clubs pay a premium price for players who have come through Football Association academies.

And with Anderson and Rogers securing their moves, could Scott and Wharton be next?

Bournemouth have already rejected a £64m bid from Chelsea for midfielder Scott as they strive to hold on to the 22-year-old, who enjoyed an impressive season on the south coast, particularly from January onwards.

Scott was selected in his first England squad last November and was then called into Thomas Tuchel's extended pre-World Cup training camp in Florida but is yet make an appearance at senior international level.

Crystal Palace midfielder Wharton has also long been a coveted player, with Liverpool, Chelsea and Manchester City all previously registering interest.

Both aged just 22, the pair highlight the plethora of English talent and potential currently available in that position.

While they may not be the best in the world right now, they are part of a group of players in their early 20s who are nearing their prime and will therefore only move clubs for peak prices.

Analysis by football finance expert Kieran Maguire

Transfer fees keep smashing records, but relative to what clubs can actually afford, they have barely moved.

Back in 1992-93, the Premier League's first season, the average club turned over £9.3m. That summer's biggest signing, Alan Shearer, cost £3.3m - 35% of average revenue.

Fast forward to 2024-25 - the latest season with full accounts published - and average Premier League revenue has ballooned to £342m - 35% of that is £121m, strikingly close to what clubs have just paid for Anderson and Rogers.

The lesson: it's not that clubs have gone transfer mad, but that they can simply afford more.

title: Two waste paper bins, one Olympic dream
description: Derived from basketball and developed by teenagers in late-Victorian England, netball is sport's great misunderstood "girly game". It could be heading to the Olympics in 2032.
fullText:
"Big up the netballers. That sport is so difficult."

Derived from basketball and developed by teenagers in late-Victorian England, netball is sport's great misunderstood "girly game".

A hotbed of feminist rebellion, its corset-shattering, myth-busting history encompasses a journey of emancipation, imagination, and healthy disdain for patriarchal expectations.

Netball is one of the Commonwealth Games' great sports, and Glasgow 2026 will thrust it into the spotlight again: sinew-stretching athleticism, pinpoint shooting, frantic end-to-end pace.

British rap superstar Stormzy ripped ligaments in his fingers, external the first time he played netball. Underestimate the physical demands, external of this non-contact sport at your peril.

But netball's leaders have an even greater platform than the Commonwealth Games in mind, not least because doubts linger over the long-term future of that event.

If ambitious efforts come to fruition, netball will be rewarded with an Olympic Games debut at Brisbane 2032, "future-proofing" it for future generations.

So yes, that sport you played in the school playground was slyly, quietly revolutionary.

But the suffragist Swede who pioneered netball in England was anything but quiet.

Martina Bergman Osterberg was a powerhouse liberal, a college owner and principal who strategically and scientifically set out to build "physically perfect" women before dispatching them to teach and create 

Netball was a mid-1890s by-product of her extraordinary physical education college operation - a fitting launchpad for a sport that has had to scuffle for attention, respect and a worthy stage, and has achieved all that thanks to decades of female steadfastness.

The by-product is pivoting into its professional era in the 21st century.

Elite netball teams are relocating from sports halls to major arenas, and players are noticing a difference in their pay packets.

Australia's Suncorp Super Netball is where the top money is for now and, while salaries are veiled in secrecy, the cream of the crop there are reputedly nudging close to the six-figure mark.

Rank-and-file in the UK-based Netball Super League are scraping into five figures and often working second jobs, although full-time marquee players fare much better.

Netball Super League is two years into a 10-year pathway to professionalism.

England were Commonwealth Games gold medallists in 2018 and World Cup runners-up in 2023. Such feats drive public interest, and attendances jumped by 45% in the first year of the professional journey.

And it remains a sport driven by women, as Osterberg would doubtless have wanted.

She was a brilliantly radical leader: demolishing the concept of men being best suited to train women, dismissing rival teaching methods, and convincing head teachers they needed her college graduates to run their physical education programmes.

A magazine account admiringly described Osterberg's students, typically in their late teens, as "teachers in embryo... destined high priestesses of a new cult".

That verdict came in 1897, towards the end of a century in which women who exercised and played sports had frequently faced disapproval.

Through her audacity and fierce self-belief, Osterberg was a prime mover in rewriting the playbook for women in British sport.

And it was not beyond her to borrow a good idea...

Osterberg caught wind of the invention of basketball by Massachusetts gym teacher James Naismith in 1891 and its subsequent rapid surge in popularity earlier than most in the UK. Her exposure to the sport possibly came on an 1893 trip to Chicago.

It took just that seed for its sister sport netball to take root - initially also going by the name of basketball.

The sport also cadged elements from a women's version of basketball featuring player zone restrictions.

America's basketball fad had been swiftly deconstructed, repurposed.

Goals were awarded instead of points, dribbling outlawed and rings with nets replaced the bins.

In 1901, netball's first set of formal rules was established and Australia and New Zealand were by now on board.

Like basketball, netball began as a nine-a-side sport. Rules diverged and were not globally standardised to seven-a-side until 1960.

A sport cannot function seriously on a world scale without such conformity, and a World Cup - launched in 1963 and contested every four years since - needed that.

Osterberg courted Britain's middle and upper classes for her college, deciding most elementary school girls were "absolutely unfit to be trained... their muscles have been starved from babyhood".

She was a disciple of the anatomy-based, apparatus-free gymnastics 'Swedish system', and this specialism defined her teaching.

Osterberg was irritated by claims women had "a faulty battery pack" that needed preserving for motherhood and wifedom, netball historian Margaret Henley told BBC Sport.

Henley, a lecturer from the University of Auckland, has studied netball deeply, both locally and globally.

"It is unique - the only sport that I have been able to find that is completely controlled, operated, designed by, played by women over its history," Henley said.

"It is a team sport that men do not play or, if they play, there's netball and there's men's netball. It's distinct from rugby and women's rugby and football and women's football. Women are the benchmark of how the game is played.

"It links it quite strongly to women's very quiet, unobtrusive emancipation, within the Commonwealth particularly.

"It's seen as being sort of a girly game because it's played by women. If you've watched a bit of netball, you'll know that there's nothing traditionally girly about the way netball's played."

Henley said netball initially encouraged teamwork, alongside having "a hell of a good time".

"The women found leadership, management skills, financial skills, where they were previously confined to the house and the domestic role," she said.

"So really it's a misunderstood hotbed of feminist advancement - because it disguised its purposes so carefully."

While staunchly feminist, Osterberg often expressed her vision in social Darwinist terms.

It was the Penny Pictorial Magazine of 1900 that painted Osterberg's Dartford campus as "the one institution in the world which has for its single aim and object the production of physically perfect women".

Netball flourished alongside hockey, tennis, cricket, cycling, swimming and dancing, while students were taught anatomy, physiology and hygiene, theory of movement, massage and medicine gymnastics.

Truly a finishing school like no other.

The gymslip - staple no-frills sportswear for girls across much of the 20th century - was even invented at the college by Osterberg's superintendent Mary Tait. Corsets were immediately banished.

As Henley said: "Taking the corset off and wearing a gym tunic was an incredibly liberating moment."

Students committed to strict diet, alongside no alcohol consumption and early-to-bed policies. Many then took the novel sport of netball away with them on graduating and taught it to their own pupils.

Osterberg died aged 65 in 1915, of cancer, and gifted the college to the English nation.

England Netball formed in 1926, making 2026 the national body's centenary year.

The Dartford college, in various guises, trained teachers until the 1980s. Its main house, Kingsfield, was being converted into apartments in 2026.

Netball meanwhile has long grown up and left home.

Its next move could be the biggest since Osterberg's day.

The sport that began in embryonic form a year before the modern Olympics launched in Athens now wants to join the greatest sporting show on Earth.

Netball reputedly has 13 million players globally. England has the most active players of all countries with three million, and Africa has the most of all continents.

Traditionally, a Commonwealth-centric sport, there is no mistaking Glasgow 2026 is a major occasion for netball.

At the same time, the Commonwealth Games is grappling for survival, amid concerns over hosting costs and growing questions about relevancy to the modern world given its foundations in Britain's colonial past.

The event remains "hugely important to netball", World Netball president Dame Liz Nicholl told BBC Sport, pointing out over 150,000 tickets were sold for netball at Birmingham 2022.

"We strongly support the Commonwealth Games and want to see it thrive," she said. "But the future of netball cannot depend on one event."

Chelsea Pitman, a Commonwealth gold medallist with England in 2018 and now head coach at Nottingham Forest, told BBC Sport: "We need it. If the Olympics becomes part of netball, [it would be] absolutely amazing. But Commonwealth Games allows netball to be part of a multi-sport, massive masterpiece of sport. There's nothing like it."

England head coach Anna Stembridge agrees, telling BBC Sport: "It's a really important part of our history... a really important part of our four-year cycle."

There's another stumbling block: netball is no longer guaranteed a Commonwealth Games platform, with only athletics and swimming now designated as core sports.

Netball is mindful of all possibilities and one alteration is that the quadrennial World Cup is going biennial from next year.

But the campaign for inclusion in the 2032 Olympics is also gathering pace, with a decision expected soon.

"Achieving Olympic inclusion would be a very important moment in the sport's history," said Dame Liz, a former Wales netball international who was UK Sport CEO from 2010 to 2019.

She believes the knock-on effects of netball getting the nod from the IOC could "future-proof it for the generations to come".

"To be in the Olympics is an ambition for every athlete and, if ever there is a chance, it is now," she said.

"We must demonstrate strong governance, global reach, compelling events and alignment with the Olympic movement's priorities.

"We believe netball has a very strong case and can make a meaningful contribution to Brisbane 2032."

From two waste paper bins and a ball in a Kent college to the Olympic stage?

To read more about the life and times of Martine Bergman Osterberg, visit The Osterberg Collection, external

Dr Margaret Henley and colleague Prof Toni Bruce were recipients of a Royal Society of New Zealand Marsden Grant (2022-27) for their study: Netball's enduring role in the intergenerational health and wellbeing of Aotearoa women

title: Ex-UFC champion Andrade working as delivery driver
description: Jessica Andrade says she will do "whatever it takes" to earn a living as she recovers from injury.
fullText:
Former UFC strawweight champion Jessica Andrade says she has been working as a delivery driver to earn extra money while she recovers from injury.

The Brazilian, 34, has not competed since losing to Loopy Godinez last August, when she injured a shoulder.

Andrade has fought 30 times in the UFC since her debut in 2013, winning 17 of her fights.

"I work as a delivery driver. I deliver meals and things like that. I just pick up orders through an app and make the deliveries," Andrade said, external on the Brazilian MMA podcast Direto de Vegas.

"I do whatever it takes. I'm not afraid of hard work. I can do just about anything."

Andrade said she has started a landscaping project at her friend's house, which involves removing rocks, putting down grass and painting fences.

"If I'm good at this it becomes one more thing I can add if I ever find myself short on cash. We need some extra money," she said.

In 2018 Andrade said she sold her UFC clothes, backpack and gloves because she was struggling to get sponsors, but added that she was "paid really well" in the promotion.

In 2023 she said she fought five times to pay for her divorce, while the following year she accused former coach Gilliard Parana of withholding money from her. Parana denied all allegations.

UFC fighter pay has been questioned by athletes and the media after boxer Conor Benn signed a reported £11m one-fight deal with Zuffa Boxing, owned by UFC president Dana White.

The UFC gives about 20% of revenue to fighter pay, while boxers get about 60% of revenue from their events.

Asked about criticism, White has pointed to UFC fighter pay rising since 2001.

title: Is 2026 the Commonwealth Games' last dance or a future-proofing revamp?
description: It's been going for 96 years - but many doubted the 2026 Commonwealth Games, which open in Glasgow on Thursday, would happen. So what could the future hold for the event?
fullText:
It is an event that has been going for 96 years.

The Commonwealth Games have provided iconic sporting moments – from Roger Bannister's win in the 'Miracle Mile' of 1954 and Eilish McColgan breaking her mum's record in 2022 to England's netball victory four years earlier in Australia.

But the 2026 Games, which open in Glasgow on Thursday, come at an intriguing time for the event. Many doubted whether they would even happen - and when they do go ahead, they will do so in a stripped-back form.

So how did we get here? And do athletes and fans still care and what could the future hold?

The 2026 Commonwealth Games were thrown into doubt when its original hosts, the Australian state of Victoria, pulled out in 2023 citing spiraling costs. That decision took even the leaders of Commonwealth Sport by surprise.

After more than a year of uncertainty, Glasgow stepped in to host the Games for the second time, following on from the highly successful event in the Scottish city in 2014.

But, with limited preparation time a significant factor, the Games' return to Glasgow a much smaller event.

There was competition across 18 sports in 2014 – a number that rose to 20 for Birmingham 2022 - but only 10 sports will feature this year.

There are expected to be around 2,000 fewer athletes and no new stadiums have been built, with the athletics events having moved from the 46,000-seater Hampden Park to the Scotstoun Stadium, which will hold just over 10,000 people.

Costs of running the event have been cut from between £700m and £800m for Birmingham 2022 and the Gold Coast Games in 2018 to £160m for Glasgow.

Whether by accident or design, organisers insist the situation has created a template for the future.

They believe a smaller, cheaper, more sustainable Commonwealth Games can last.

"I don't think we have ever been stronger," Katie Sadleir, chief executive of Commonwealth Sport, told BBC Sport.

"We took a hard look at ourselves and realised we needed to reset and reimagine this Games so it is an attractive proposition for our members."

Crucially, the Commonwealth Games have already secured the Indian city of Ahmedabad as host for 2030, when the number of sports is expected rise to between 15 and 18.

Several countries have also expressed interest in hosting in 2034. New Zealand has done so publicly while Nigeria remains interested after challenging Ahmedabad's bid for 2030.

Is Glasgow ready for the Commonwealth Games - and does the city care?

All you need to know about Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games

Having begun in 1930 as the British Empire Games, the Commonwealth Games face questions about their place and relevance in the modern world.

England weightlifter Emily Campbell told the BBC the Games are "one of the best things that came out of colonisation" while three-time Olympic swimming gold medallist Tom Dean insists they can be a "force for good" despite the difficult past.

"It is even more reason to push the Commonwealths into the spotlight because it shows the best side of sport," he told BBC Sport.

However, the Glasgow edition has been hit by the absence of star names.

Injuries, such as those to Olympic gold-medal winning gymnast Max Whitlock and Australia's Olympic backstroke champion Kaylee McKeown, are unavoidable and unfortunate.

But other athletes have opted to prioritise different events.

Olympic 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson is skipping Glasgow in favour of the European Athletics Championships, which start in Birmingham on 10 August and are arguably the bigger track and field event this summer.

Dina Asher-Smith, the fastest British woman in history, and Olympic 100m champion Julien Alfred of St Lucia have also pulled out. Canadian swimming superstar Summer McIntosh is another notable absentee.

But it is not all doom and gloom.

Josh Kerr, fresh from breaking the mile world record, will return to home soil to race for Scotland, as will McColgan.

Katerina Johnson-Thompson is chasing her third Commonwealth heptathlon title in a row and Adam Ramsay-Peaty returns to the same pool in which he won two golds in 2014.

The Commonwealth Games remain a pinnacle for sports like netball and there is also an increased focus on Para-sport, with this the most integrated Games ever.

Six of the 10 sports include Para-competitions with a Commonwealth Games record 47 medal events. Golds, silvers and bronzes won in Para-events will, unlike with other multi-sport events, count towards the overall medal total.

"There are not many opportunities for the integration of multi-sports [events]," Paralympic swimming champion Brock Whiston told BBC Sport.

"Without the Commonwealth Games, the smaller countries don't get the chance to show how powerful that integration is.

"The Commonwealth Games is vital to ensuring there are equal opportunities."

English swimmer James Guy, another Olympic champion who will be at his fourth Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, told BBC Sport the event remains a crucial part of an athlete's development.

"It is a bit easier to make than the GB team," he said.

"It is a really good way to get on there, get on a relay and get a medal and that is really important for development so the younger guys and girls can progress."

Five big names to look out for at Glasgow 2026

As ever, another major factor in assessing the potential longevity of the Commonwealth Games will be the response from fans.

Around 1.3 million tickets were sold in 2014 – a figure that will certainly not be matched this time.

Sales have been strongest for athletics and cycling but tickets remain available for all sports across the 11 days of competition.

There is also a significant change in the UK this year with the BBC, who had provided coverage for 18 Commonwealth Games in a row, being replaced by TNT Sports as the live television broadcaster.

While radio coverage remains on BBC Radio 5 Live, it means there will be no free-to-air live TV coverage.

That makes cutting through to the general public more challenging at a crucial time.

The next 11 days remain significant for the Games' future.

title: Every Princeton player drafted by the Golden State Warriors
description: Let's take a look at every player who has been drafted by the Warriors out of Princeton.
fullText:
The Golden State Warriors have put together their teams through a variety of ways over the years, yet few approaches have proven as successful as the NBA draft. More than anything else, the most talented players to represent the Warriors have arrived in Golden State either by being selected directly in the annual draft or through trades executed on that same night.

The Golden State Warriors have taken many of their top stars through the draft, but have also landed a number of notable players over the years as well. From tiny colleges to blue blood programs, these alumni have contributed significantly to the team's roster over the years. So, we chose to take a closer look at which Dubs came from which schools over the years.

So without further ado, let's take a look at every player who has been drafted by the Warriors out of Princeton.

title: What the New England Patriots Recent Roster Moves Mean Going Forward
description: Signing Riley Wilson and Mitch Van Vooren was one of the major roster transactions for the Patriots as training camp kicks off.
fullText:
The New England Patriots made some rather interesting moves as things ramp up for training camp. The Patriots added two new players to their roster in tight end Mitch Van Vooren and linebacker and UDFA Riley Wilson.

These moves come at an interesting time, with an aging linebacker corps and some backups on the current roster itching for a 53-man roster spot, as well as a dire need for a TE2 or TE3 to fit into the offensive system ever since Julian Hill went on IR earlier this offseason.

Both additions give depth and give Mike Vrabel a lot to look at as training camp takes shape for when it is time to cut things down to 53 men.

Let's start with Van Vooren, who is a multifaceted athlete. Sure, he did not get an invite to the Combine back in 2025, but let's not discredit anything here. The tight end is 6 feet 5, weighs 255 pounds, and was once a track star in college.

Van Vooren set school records in the 400-meter hurdles at both St. Norbert and Marquette. He followed that up by being voted to the All-Big East Outdoor team in 2022 and named first-team All-American in 2023.

There's clearly something here the Patriots could make use of even if he's just a spot to fill on the roster.

Wilson tested well on the 40-yard dash this past spring at the NFL Combine. The linebacker ran it at 4.57 seconds at his pro day. It was the eighth-fastest time for a linebacker at the Combine this year.

Wilson brings in a lot of versatility at his position. He played both edge rusher and off-ball linebacker during his college career at Hawaii, Montana, and the University of Arizona. His speed and quick movements helped him play both roles.

Let's also discuss the financials with these two.

As we saw with the Isaiah Iton signing earlier this week, the signing of these two additions should be similar, without any real cap hit (although there are not many public details on this yet).

One-year deals that circle the $885,000 minimum salary are usually what we see here. Also, no guarantees are expected to be part of the pacts, and they have no impact on the Patriots' salary cap (the NFL's Top 51 Rule).

Follow Tyler for more Patriots content

Check out the Patriots Roundtable Podcast and like and subscribe

Patriots Roundtable also offers a fan community and message board. We’d love to have you join us to talk all things Patriots. Click the “Join” button at the top of the page to join our community for free.

title: Houston Rockets jersey history No. 22 - Alonzo Bradley (1977-80)
description: During his time suiting up for the Rockets, Bradley wore only jersey Nos. 30 and 22 and put up 4.4 points and 1.5 rebounds per game.
fullText:
The Houston Rockets have had players donning a total of 52 different jersey numbers (and have one not part of any numerical series for Houston assistant coach and general manager Carroll Dawson) since their founding at the start of the 1967-68 season, worn by just under 500 players in the course of Rockets history.

To honor all of the players who wore those numbers over the decades, Rockets Wire is covering the entire history of jersey numbers and the players who wore them since the founding of the team all those years ago right up to the present day.

With seven of those jerseys now retired to honor some of the greatest Rockets of all time to wear those jerseys, there is a lot of history to cover.

And for today's article, we will continue with the fourth of eight who wore the No. 22, forward alum Alonzo Bradley. After ending his college career at Texas Southern, Bradley was picked up with the 29th overall selection of the 1977 NBA Draft by the Indiana Pacers.

The Utica, Mississippi native played the first three seasons of his pro career with Houston after a trade before he began his rookie season, leaving the league as a player afterward.

During his time suiting up for the Rockets, Bradley wore only jersey Nos. 30 and 22 and put up 4.4 points and 1.5 rebounds per game.

All stats and data courtesy of Basketball Reference.

This article originally appeared on Rockets Wire: Houston Rockets jersey history No. 22 - Alonzo Bradley (1977-80)

title: Loopy Godinez wants Virna Jandiroba rematch at Noche UFC
description: Loppy Godinez would love to avenge her 2024 loss to Virna Jandiroba at this year's Noche UFC.
fullText:
Loopy Godinez has never had a career rematch, and she'd like to experience one starting in 2026.

Godinez, a contender in the UFC women's strawweight division, would like to run things back with recent UFC title challenger Virna Jandiroba. Godinez (14-6 MMA, 9-6 UFC) lost to Jandiroba (23-4 MMA, 9-4 UFC) back in 2024. The Mexican fighter lost a unanimous decision, which put an end to her four-fight winning streak at the time. Over two years later, Godinez is ready to settle the score.

"I would personally love to have a rematch against Jandiroba," Godinez told Hablemos MMA in Spanish. "I think all the top contenders are already booked, so I would like to get a rematch against Jandiroba."

Godinez not only has a name in mind but also a date and location. She'd like to be part of this year's Noche UFC – which goes down on Sept. 12 in Glendale, Ariz. Both Godinez and Jandiroba haven't fought since April.

"I would love to fight at Noche," Godinez said. "The timing would be perfect. Fighting at Noche is incredible. I fought in the one the year before The Sphere, and to be honest, it was incredible. I loved the energy, and I honestly felt like I was in Mexico. The UFC did a great job with it."

Godinez looks to get back in the win column. She last fought at UFC 327 in April, where she was submitted by former UFC title challenger Tatiana Suarez. The defeat snapped a two-fight winning streak for Godinez.

This article originally appeared on MMA Junkie: Loopy Godinez wants Virna Jandiroba rematch at Noche UFC

The top rivalry out on the West Coast between the UCLA Bruins and Gonzaga Bulldogs will officially return on Dec. 5 at the Honda Center in Anaheim, California. The Zags are looking to make it nine victories over the Bruins in their 12 all-time matchups.

title: Barcelona Prepare for Ferran Torres Exit Amid PSG Interest
description: Barcelona expect Ferran Torres to leave this summer with PSG leading the race. Luis Enrique has personally called the striker to present the Parisian project.
fullText:
Among PSG's attacking leads, Barcelona striker Ferran Torres is causing his current club to fear a departure, with the club becoming less confident about a possible contract extension.

Recently crowned world champion, Torres must discuss his future with Barcelona in the coming days, once the World Cup title won with Spain has finished being celebrated, but the Catalan club is already beginning to come to terms with this case, according to the local sports outlet Sport. The hoped-for extension is even seen as complicated, and the initial plan to wait until September now seems completely out of the question.

Barcelona has postponed discussions for this extension for months and did not see the interest from other clubs in Torres coming, 26 years old and under contract only until June 2027. Premier League clubs are on his trail, but above all PSG. As L'Equipe explained yesterday, Luis Enrique called him personally to present the Parisian project, his role within it, and the striker was rather convinced by the presentation.

On the Barcelona side, they now expect the striker to depart, and his current silence is seen as a sign of an imminent departure. And if Torres intends to stay in Catalonia, he will have to make financial efforts because Barcelona absolutely cannot compete with the Parisian club on this point; quite the contrary. Not to mention that extending the player's contract would force Barcelona to pay his former club, Manchester City, $9 million in bonuses.

What also does not work in Barcelona's favor is its attitude in another case, that of Julian Alvarez. For weeks, the Catalan executives have been bending over backward to bring in the Argentine, and Torres has clearly noticed that he is not considered in the same way, but rather as the replacement for the possible recruit and nothing more.

Barcelona is waiting for the player's will, which is always the major point of the case, but they are already preparing to negotiate a transfer with PSG, the big favorite in this case. A reunion with Luis Enrique, his first national team coach, is entirely conceivable.

Elsewhere, Lucas Digne is undergoing his PSG medical as his return nears completion. The club also confirmed academy extensions for Thomas Leyssales and Jean-Francois Vulliez.

After being linked with the move for several days, Digne's transfer to PSG is close to being completed, with the player undergoing his medical to finalize his return.

France's World Cup campaign ended last Saturday with a wild 6-4 loss to England, and PSG's French contingent is about to welcome a new member. As reported over the past few days, Lucas Digne is set to complete his return to Paris, more than 10 years after his first unsuccessful spell at the club.

title: Cincinnati Bengals to induct Bob Trumpy, James Brooks into Ring of Honor
description: Jul. 22—Former Cincinnati Bengals tight end Bob Trumpy and running back James Brooks will be going into the organization's Ring of Honor this year. The Bengals announced Wednesday, July 22 that Trumpy and Brooks were selected as the 2026 Ring of Honor inductees, based on a vote by season ticket holders from a ballot featuring 12 individuals who have played a significant role in the franchise's ...
fullText:
Jul. 22—Former Cincinnati Bengals tight end Bob Trumpy and running back James Brooks will be going into the organization's Ring of Honor this year.

The Bengals announced Wednesday, July 22 that Trumpy and Brooks were selected as the 2026 Ring of Honor inductees, based on a vote by season ticket holders from a ballot featuring 12 individuals who have played a significant role in the franchise's history and tradition.

Trumpy, who played for the Bengals from 1968 through 1977, and Brooks (1984-91) will join Ken Anderson, Willie Anderson, Paul Brown, Isaac Curtis, Corey Dillon, Boomer Esiason, Chad Johnson, Dave Lapham, Tim Krumrie, Anthony Muñoz, Lemar Parrish and Ken Riley in the Bengals Ring of Honor.

The induction ceremony will take place during halftime of the game against the Tennessee Titans on Nov. 1.

Trumpy was a 12th-round draft pick of the Bengals in their inaugural 1968 season and notably scored the team's first-ever receiving touchdown. He went on to play in 128 games with 120 starts over the next 10 seasons and set the standard for future players at his position. Among all tight ends in team history, he has the most receiving yards (4,600), receiving touchdowns (35), yards per reception (15.4) and Pro Bowl selections (four).

"In my opinion, during his time, Bob was the best receiving tight end in the league," Bengals President Mike Brown said in a press release. "He was a top-level player. He had a long stride. He had good hands. Because the safeties couldn't stay with him, he became a productive downfield threat for us. He had something that drove him to be good, and he became a great player."

Brooks remains the franchise's career record holder for average yards per carry (4.8; minimum 500 attempts), while ranking second in team history in rushing yards (6,447) and tied for fifth in rushing touchdowns (37) in his eight seasons. A versatile and complete running back, Brooks also contributed 3,012 career receiving yards to give him 9,459 yards from scrimmage, which is the fourth-most in Bengals history. He also holds three of the team's top 10 highest scrimmage yardage totals in a season, highlighted by a franchise-record 1,773 yards in 1986.

"James was one of the toughest players we ever had," Brown said in the press release. "He was a good runner, and not only was he a good catch out of the backfield, he knew how to get yards after he made the catch. And he was quick. Extremely hard to cover on the goal line. But the thing the numbers don't say that needs being said is he was an exceptional pass protector. It's good he's recognized. He deserves it."

title: C.J. Stroud Gets Blunt Take Before Texans Vets Report for Training Camp
description: He has a lot to prove. The post C.J. Stroud Gets Blunt Take Before Texans Vets Report for Training Camp appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
If C.J. Stroud does not show significant improvement, an opportunity that could take until the postseason to arrive in the eyes of some, the Houston Texans quarterback will see the scrutiny around him intensify.

He has already endured it all offseason.

As the Texans return for training camp, with veterans due in on July 28, no one on the roster is facing more pressure than the 2023 Offensive Rookie of the Year.

Houston Texans quarterback C.J. Stroud will be under the microscope until his future is resolved.

The Texans have spoken highly of Stroud this offseason, but their relative inaction regarding his long-term future–he is signed through 2027, thanks to his fifth-year option–has left the fourth-year QB facing a reality that belies his standout rookie season.

There is a widespread perception that Stroud has regressed over the past two seasons, an argument often presented in a vacuum without noting the Texans’ other offensive roster woes.

In the end, Stroud must bounce back and prove he can handle adversity better.

“It’s a big prove-it year. We talked about this and had a little fun with it last week: It’s just a haircut, but it’s showing you that he’s trying to do something a little bit different. And by all accounts, that sounds [like] what he’s doing this offseason,” NFL Network’s Jane Slater said on “The Insiders” podcast on July 22. “The big topic was obviously that perplexing and frustrating game in New England.

“4 interceptions, a pick-6, completing just 43% of his passes. His QBR was 22.2, fifth lowest in the playoffs last season, third worst in the Texans’ playoff history. And for context, if you just spiked the ball in every snap, your QBR is 39.6.”

Stroud has not shied away from his shortcomings in the postseason, nor has he blamed any of his teammates.

However, he was not the Texans’ only issue in the postseason.

Stroud was without his top wide receiver (Nico Collins) and right tackle (Trent Brown) from the outset against the Patriots. His usual starting left guard (Tytus Howard) played out of position with backup (Jarrett Patterson) in his spot, and top tight end (Dalton Schultz) got hurt, too.

C.J. Stroud drew encouraging feedback during the offseason program.

Stroud drew rave reviews during the offseason program, from head coach DeMeco Ryans and teammates, who lauded the work that he put in to go along with his new look.

Slater noted a key difference is that Stroud battled through an injury last offseason.

“I asked a few folks in Houston how Stroud has responded this offseason, particularly with the contract situation, and they said he’s responding well,” Slater said, noting that Stroud worked out with teammates Jayden Higgins and Jaylin Noel this offseason.

“I was told he’s looked sharp. In fact, one person told me, this is the best offseason he’s had. So, they’re really hoping that that translates into training camp. It’s also helped a little bit . . . that they’re at least putting some attention on that offensive line. Now, his numbers for getting sacked were, I think, in the 23-area compared to 56 the year before.”

The Texans revamped their offensive line for the second straight offseason, and added running back David Montgomery to elevate their rushing attack.

Still, the pressure is on Stroud to get the Texans to their goals and secure his future.

“Small improvement for him there,” Slater said on Stroud’s decreased sack rate, which set a new career low. “But he looked like he was under duress at times, and he does need to make some better decisions in the pocket; that was also an issue for him.”

Houston Texans offensive coordinator Nick Caley looks on in a game against the Tennessee Titans.

Stroud still needs those around him to step up to be the best version of himself, and that starts with Texans offensive coordinator Nick Caley.

According to Slater, there is optimism heading into his second season calling plays.

“Nick Caley, this will be his second year as the OC. And so, I’m told everyone’s a little bit more comfortable with him, and they’re settling in. So, hopefully, some of these pieces around CJ. Stroud are going to step up.

“When you look at this defense, . . . they cannot waste what they’ve got on that side of the ball. They absolutely need not only C.J. Stroud, but the rest of this team to step up. Because it’s just unfortunate–when you look at some of their efforts and the play of this team–that they’re finding themselves in back-to-back seasons, eliminated in the divisional game.”

It has actually been three straight seasons of letdowns in the Divisional Round for Stroud and the Texans. That essentially sets the bar there for the former No. 2 overall pick.

Like HEAVY's content? Be sure to follow us.

This article was originally published on HEAVY

The post C.J. Stroud Gets Blunt Take Before Texans Vets Report for Training Camp appeared first on HEAVY.

fullText:

title: "My Character and My Family": 5-Star RB Who Turned Down Ohio State Issues Strong Statement After Tennessee Commitment
description: David Gabriel Georges finally ended one of the biggest recruiting battles in the 2027 class by committing to Tennessee over Ohio State and Ole Miss. But while he celebrated his decision, the 5-star RB also felt the need to respond to what he described as false narratives, misinformation, and unfair assumptions.
fullText:
David Gabriel Georges finally ended one of the biggest recruiting battles in the 2027 class by committing to Tennessee over Ohio State and Ole Miss. But while he celebrated his decision, the 5-star RB also felt the need to respond to what he described as false narratives, misinformation, and unfair assumptions.

“I also feel it is important to address something,” David Gabriel Georges wrote in a statement posted on social media. “What should have been one of the most meaningful and joyful moments of my life has, at times, been overshadowed by negative media coverage, false narratives, and unfair assumptions about both my character and my family.”

Georges added that he understands the passion surrounding college football. However, “any form of harassment and the spread of misinformation is simply unnecessary and uncalled for.” He also made it clear that the final call belonged to him.

“This decision was mine and was made through careful thought, prayer, and countless conversations with those I trust most,” he continued. “I respectfully ask that my decision be honored and that people refrain from making assumptions or spreading false information.”

Those comments came after weeks of speculation surrounding his recruitment. Reports and online chatter suggested that NIL money had become a major factor in his commitment, with claims ranging from a seven-figure valuation to alleged Adidas connections involving both his high school and family.

None of those claims have been substantiated by David Gabriel Georges. And when he explained why Tennessee won out, his answer was much simpler than the headlines surrounding his recruitment.

“Just being around those people and talking with the coach, I just felt like it would be a great place, a great fit,” he said. “I enjoyed practice and having fun.”

The decision surprised some recruiting observers, especially after several experts had previously predicted Ohio State would land him before shifting their picks to Tennessee shortly before his announcement. As Eleven Warriors’ Dan Hope reported, Ohio State couldn’t outdo the Vols in financial terms despite going all in.

“Ohio State reportedly offered DGG an NIL deal worth more than $1 million,” he wrote. “In the end, however, the Volunteers reportedly offered Gabriel George a deal in the range of $2 million per year… And in this case, Tennessee’s ultra-aggressive financial offer, as well as promises of competing for a starting job right away, won out in the end.”

Now, the Vols are getting much more than a headline recruit. David Gabriel Georges, originally from Quebec and now starring at Baylor School in Chattanooga, is ranked as the nation’s No. 9 overall prospect by Rivals, the No. 2 RB in the class, and Tennessee’s top-ranked player.

DGG rushed for 1,749 yards and 27 touchdowns during his junior season, highlighted by a remarkable semifinal performance in which he piled up more than 400 rushing yards and seven touchdowns. He also earned Tennessee Mr. Football honors for the second straight year.

It’s another massive recruiting win for Tennessee. But for David Gabriel Georges, it was the chance to remind everyone that behind every recruiting story is a teenager and a family asking for the same thing – to show some respect while they make one of the biggest decisions of their lives.
