title: Five Hacks Every Xteink User Should Know
description: Unlock your pocket-sized e-reader's full potential.
fullText:
Most people who read e-books do so on a phone or a Kindle, and that's fine. But for the more exacting (if not adventurous) digital reading enthusiasts out there, I recommend exploring life outside of Amazon's ecosystem—and I particularly recommend doing so with one of the tiny e-readers made by Xteink, a Chinese tech company with an unfortunate name but some great devices.

Out of the box, an Xteink X3, X4, or X4 Pro can seem a little finicky, or even intimidating. But if you've mastered the basics of navigating their clunky operating systems and loading up a few books, you'll quickly find they are both magically portable and a joy for distraction-free reading. And once you're a convert, you can use these tips and hacks to get even more out of your impossibly wee e-reader.

While getting e-book formatting to match what you'd find in a printed book has always been a challenge, the native software that ships with all Xteink devices handles it particularly poorly. While you certainly can read EPUB files loaded onto them, they may show up with awkward formatting, such as unnecessary spacing between paragraphs or missing indents. Heck, these devices still can't display text in bold or italics, never mind if you're reading something with embedded charts or images.

You can solve for this by converting your books to a proprietary format called XTC before loading them onto your e-reader. Rather than a free-flowing text document, an XTC file is essentially a series of bitmap images of the original e-book. All images, stylized headers, and text formatting will thus be preserved, with a few downsides: The image can look slightly less crisp, and the file will be a lot larger—50MB or more, depending on the book, versus 2MB or so for most EPUBs.

There are a few ways to transform your EPUBs into XTC files, but the easiest is this web converter, which allows you to customize the font choice, page orientation, text alignment, and other factors, then download the finished result to sideload onto your device.

If converting to XTC is too cumbersome or limiting for you, you have another option: Replace the Xteink software entirely. Xteink devices run on dead-simple ESP32 processors, and they are mostly open and easily hackable—meaning you can overwrite the stock firmware without the need to jailbreak them first.

If you think that sounds onerous, it isn't: In less than a year since the Xteink X4 launched, the device has built up a huge online following of tech-heads and tinkerers, and they've not only created a custom firmware for Xteink devices—called CrossPoint Reader—they've built a website with a tool that allows you to flash your device with the new software with a single click. (Note: As of this writing, CrossPoint is not yet available for the new X4 Pro, though it is in development.)

After upgrading it to CrossPoint, you'll suddenly find your Xteink e-reader a lot more pleasant to use, with more straightforward menus and options and a host of improvements to the e-reading experience, including a wider selection of fonts and more support for customizing your margins, line spacing, and more.

But you need not stop there. There are dozens of "forks" of CrossPoint built on the same basic platform but offering different tools and custom options. Flashing with these takes one extra step—you'll need to download the .bin files from GitHub first, then choose "Custom .bin" from the CrossPoint Flash Firmware page—but it's still a fairly foolproof process. Here are a few fun ones to consider, or you can browse the entire list:

CrossInk: This popular CrossPoint variant emphasizes improved fonts and adds basic reading stats.

CrossWordle: This option adds a Wordle-esque game to the CrossPoint firmware.

CPR-vCodex: This CrossPoint fork is all about the stats, offering in-depth, granular tools for tracking and gamifying your reading.

CrossPet: This one adds a Tamagotchi-like virtual chicken to your e-reader for some reason.

Biscuit: Aiming to turn your Xteink device into more than an e-reader, Biscuit uses tile-based navigation and adds features like a Bluetooth scanner, a simple chat app, a password manager, calculator and flashcard apps, and some games (including a passable Tetris clone).

Even with custom firmware, it can be tough to get your e-book files looking how you want them to on Xteink devices, but another one-click tool can help with that. Calibre is a popular free program for managing your e-book library on your computer, so you can customize your files before transferring them to your device, and a group of developers have built a plugin for the Calibre app that will automatically optimize your EPUB files for use in CrossPoint.

To get started, download the .zip file on this page. Open Calibre, then in the Preferences menu, choose Advanced > Plugins > Load plugin from file and select the .zip file to install it. Restart Calibre. Add the tool to the toolbar by going to Preferences > Interface > Toolbars & menus. To use it to optimize your EPUBs, select one or more of them in Calibre, then click "EPUB Workflow" in the toolbar. Check the options you want to tweak, from shrinking images to removing extraneous code to repairing any errors in the file, then click OK.

From there, you can use another Calibre plugin to send books directly to the device. To use it, head to this link and download the .zip file and repeat the process above to install it. On your Xteink device running CrossPoint, connect to the same wifi as your computer, then go to File transfer > Connect to Calibre library. Your device will now appear in Calibre, and you can use the "Send to device" button there to load files onto it directly.

Xteink devices are great for reading on the go, but maybe you prefer to use something with a larger screen when you're not out and about. Well, there's an easy way to sync your reading progress between devices, provided you've got another e-reader that can run KOReader, a free, open-source app specifically designed for e-ink displays. KOReader syncs automatically with Kobo devices, but you can also sideload it onto an Android device, whether that's your phone or an e-ink Android tablet like the Boox Palma. On iPhone, you can install the Readest app from the App Store; it has a KOReader sync plugin. (There's no easy sync-to-Kindle option, unless you want to jailbreak that device.)

Syncing your progress is pretty simple—you just have to have the same EPUB file loaded on both devices, and you need to be signed in to the same KOsync account (and server) on both of them. The CrossPoint Reader site has a page where you can create a KOsync login: Choose a username and password, and leave the server as the default. Next, log into KOReader Sync on your Android device (the process will vary by the device or app you're using), and use those same credentials on your Xteink device by heading to Settings > System > KOReader Sync. Once you've logged in properly, syncing your progress is as simple as heading back to that KOReader Sync menu on your Xteink device and choosing "Authenticate."

Xteink devices are great because they use an open platform that doesn't tie you down to any one retailer (like Kindle yolks you to Amazon). The downside is that it can be a lot harder to find books to read on it, since retailers like Amazon and Barnes & Noble usually laden their e-book files with Digital Rights Management (DRM) software that will prevent you from loading them on just any device.

I discuss the "DRM problem" 

Fortunately, there are a few places you can source public domain e-books that don't look like they're worth what you paid for them (i.e., nothing). Here are a few I suggest the next time you're in the mood to read one of the classics:

Global Grey E-Books: This site is a labor of love for the woman who runs it; she personally formats all the EPUBs to ensure they are easily readable. I don't love the AI-generated covers she favors, but it's a minor gripe given she does it all for free.

Standard Ebooks: According to this donation-supported e-book repository's "About" page, Standard Ebooks is "a volunteer-driven effort to produce a collection of high-quality, carefully formatted, accessible, open source, and free public domain ebooks that meet or exceed the quality of commercially produced ebooks." I'd say they've succeeded on that score: Every book I've sampled from their collection looks indistinguishable from a commercially produced e-book. The covers are even pretty nice, created using classical works of art also in the public domain.

title: How Garmin's Acquisition of TrainingPeaks Might Affect Your Fitness Routine
description: For now, Garmin's messaging is all about "enhancing the ecosystem."
fullText:
This morning, Garmin announced it has acquired TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic. These platforms are major players in the endurance and strength-training world, primarily for the quality of their training plans. This news is already sparking a lot of speculation—and skepticism—among athletes and coaches who've built their training around these tools. Here's what this acquisition might mean for the way you train.

According to Garmin's official press release, this acquisition “strengthens Garmin's fitness ecosystem with customizable coaching experiences for endurance, strength, and performance-focused athletes across every stage of their fitness journey.” The deal covers TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic only, not all of parent company Peaksware Holdings. This is a Garmin-specific focus on its endurance and strength assets, rather than a sale of the entire parent company.

TrainingPeaks is best known for its training plans, logs, and coaching tools, but this deal is drawing attention to other features and perks. For instance, Garmin likely has its eye on TrainingPeaks Virtual, a nifty indoor cycling platform.

It's also worth noting that TrainingPeaks owns the trademarks behind some popular metrics for serious cyclists, like Training Stress Score, Normalized Power, and Intensity Factor. As of right now, other companies like Wahoo, Hammerhead, and TrainerRoad license those terms for use in their own products—we'll see if the Garmin deal impacts this. Garmin already dominates the hardware layer with its watches, bike computers, and sensors that generate the data. What it hasn't dominated is the planning and coaching layer that sits on top of that.

There's also a strength-training angle with TrainHeroic. Garmin is clearly trying to get firmer footing in the strength and hybrid training worlds, and buying up an established strength-programming platform is a much faster path than building one from scratch.

This is where the community's nervousness comes in, and it's not unfounded. As of right now, TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic connects to more than 100 devices and apps, including hardware from Garmin's direct competitors like Wahoo, Coros, Polar, Suunto, and Apple. Garmin now has incentive to make that platform less accessible. Whether it acts on that incentive is unclear, so there are three broad scenarios people are speculating about:

Best case: Garmin leaves TrainingPeaks largely alone as a standalone product, keeps it multi-brand, and maybe integrates some select TrainingPeaks features into Connect+.

Middle case: Garmin treats TrainingPeaks the way Strava has treated Runna since acquiring it, which might look like a sort of combo-deal subscription.

Worst case: Garmin absorbs TrainingPeaks' features and utterly paywalls them. Existing users lose years of workout history, race notes, and coaching plans.

Nothing in today's announcement addresses these fears directly. For now, Garmin's messaging is all about "enhancing the ecosystem."

Acquisitions like this typically take months (sometimes longer) before any product changes roll out, and Garmin has given no timeline. Here are some longer-term questions worth watching:

Will non-Garmin device support go away?

Will Garmin's strength training finally get better?

Will pricing for either platform change?

Garmin is already one of the biggest names in athlete devices and data; today's acquisition expands the company's reach more into coaching and structured training plans.

Maybe TrainingPeaks' best training tools make it into Connect+, Garmin's strength training gets a long-overdue upgrade, and everybody wins. Or maybe a beloved independent tool gets slowly absorbed and degraded. For now, all we can do is keep an eye on whether TrainingPeaks' device integrations with Garmin competitors stay intact, and what happens to pricing.

title: How to Take Creatine for the (Possible) Mental Health Benefits
description: The evidence on using creatine for mental health is mixed, but promising.
fullText:
Creatine is best known as a supplement for people who hit the gym: Take a scoop of creatine powder every day, and you may find yourself able to work a little harder. But creatine is also being studied for its mental health benefits. Is it worth taking just for those? Maybe.

Creatine is a natural chemical that our bodies use for energy production in cells. If you remember learning about ATP as an energy source (and how mitochondria, the “powerhouse of the cell,” turn food into ATP), you already know a little bit about this. When ATP is used, creatine phosphate can recharge that ATP (replacing its spent phosphate) for some quick extra energy.

This is why it’s so useful in the gym: if you’ve just done nine bench presses and want to do a tenth, but your muscles are out of energy, having a little extra creatine in your muscle cells might give you just enough oomph to finish that last rep. Over time, you’ll end up doing more work, and thus a person who has a lot of creatine in their muscles will probably end up a little bit stronger than a person who has less.

Our bodies make some creatine, but we also get creatine from the food we eat (especially meat). Creatine supplements, available as powder or pills, can add to our internal creatine stores. That’s why it’s a popular supplement among people who do a lot of strength training.

Muscle cells aren’t the only cells that use creatine—pretty much every cell in our body needs energy, and creatine is part of the system that provides it. So it’s worth asking whether supplementing creatine might help other areas of our health.

This is where the evidence gets a lot less definitive. To give you a sense of the state of the science, I took a look at Examine.com’s database on studies relating to creatine supplementation. It records “A” level evidence for creatine’s benefits to strength and to power (the ability to use strength in quick bursts). Creatine gets a “B” for growing the size of your muscles and for some tests of memory. Many of the other studied outcomes are duds, with lots of “C” and “D” ratings as you scroll down the table. These lower ratings don’t mean that creatine is useless, just that there’s no clear, well-understood benefit that’s been solidly established. Scientists are still working on these questions.

When it comes to cognitive benefits, the evidence is mixed, but many researchers feel it’s promising. Many influencers, biohackers, and wellness enthusiasts take an optimistic view, supplementing creatine themselves (and often recommending or selling it) in hopes the benefits turn out to be real.

A 2024 review of 16 studies found that creatine seems to improve memory, attention, and processing speed. The strength of the evidence wasn’t great, though: the authors write that the evidence for benefits to memory is only “moderate” and for other benefits the evidence is of “low certainty.” Some outcomes that were studied, including overall cognitive performance, didn’t seem to be improved by creatine at all.

A 2025 review highlighted the disappointing results so far, while pointing out that we know creatine supplementation does actually increase brain levels of creatine. In other words, it’s doing something, but we haven’t entirely figured out how these benefits would work, if they in fact do.

Both reviews noted that creatine’s effect on brain performance may depend on who is taking it. People who have less creatine in their body to start with—such as vegetarians, who don’t get much in their diet—might benefit from supplementation even if omnivores don’t. Older adults may benefit more than younger, healthier people, and it’s possible the benefits of creatine show themselves best in people who are stressed.

Sleep deprivation may be a type of stress that creatine can help with. A 2024 study found that a single mega-dose of creatine helped cognitive performance in people who were sleep deprived. That dose was huge, though: the equivalent of 24 grams for a 150-pound person. (A normal dose of creatine is 5 grams each day.) I’m not saying you should take creatine if you’re feeling foggy after a late night, but I’m certainly going to think about it next time that happens to me.

Creatine is one of the best-studied consumer supplements out there, and the safety profile is good. That’s why so many people take it: even if you’re not sure it’s working, there’s no significant harm in taking it anyway. Creatine isn’t linked with any serious health issues at normal doses, and it’s pretty affordable. For example, this Nutricost tub works out to 20 cents per daily serving (if you’re taking five grams each day).

The main risk of taking creatine is that it can cause stomach upset, especially in larger doses. That could be a problem if you’re intending to try a 24-gram dose, or one of the other dosing schemes from cognitive studies. People who take creatine for cognitive benefits often aim for 10 grams a day. You can split that into five grams in the morning and five in the evening, if taking 10 at once doesn’t agree with you.

Now, there’s one other risk of creatine that people commonly talk about online, but it doesn’t have any solid evidence behind it. That’s the idea that creatine may accelerate hair loss in people (usually men) who are already prone to what’s sometimes called male pattern baldness. This idea comes from looking at the biochemical pathways involved in creatine metabolism and in hormonal changes related to testosterone, and finding a place where they intersect.

Researchers flagged this as something worth looking into—maybe creatine possibly could accelerate hair loss, but the internet ran with “creatine causes hair loss” as if it were a known fact. In truth, no human study has verified this link, and a study designed to look for it found the opposite. It concluded: “This study was the first to directly assess hair follicle health following creatine supplementation, providing strong evidence against the claim that creatine contributes to hair loss.”

If you’re interested in taking creatine for mental health benefits, the rules are the same as for people who are taking it for muscular benefits—except that you may want a higher dose.

As mentioned, there’s not a specific number of grams we know you need to take for brain benefits (because we aren’t totally sure whether there are brain benefits for the average person). Ten grams per day seems to be a popular choice. Five grams is usually recommended for muscular benefits. For the gym benefits, you want to take your creatine consistently every day (don’t skip taking it on rest days), and it doesn’t matter what time of day. For brain benefits, the same rules of thumb seem reasonable.

Sometimes people take a “loading” dose for their first week or so, taking larger doses (often four times as much as the regular dose) to build up the body’s stores of creatine faster. This is optional: taking a regular dose for a few weeks does the same thing to your body as taking a loading dose for one week. I’d say most people these days probably skip the loading phase, because of the risk of stomach upset with large doses, and because we know the loading phase isn’t necessary.

Finally, people often ask what kind of creatine to get. And the answer is simple: creatine monohydrate is the best-studied and known to be effective. Happily, it is also the cheapest and the easiest to find. I wouldn’t worry about the brand; it’s all the same stuff. Personally I like taking creatine in capsules, since it’s more convenient than mixing powder into a drink, but the powder is cheaper and just as good.

title: Five big names to look out for at Commonwealth Games
description: These are the stars to look out for across the 10 sports at the 2026 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow.
fullText:
With 215 golds up for grabs at the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games, highlighting just five medal hopefuls is no easy feat.

Scotland's largest city is set to welcome more than 3,000 athletes from 10 sports, all of whom have the ambition of leaving with a slab of metal resting on their chest.

For some, a podium-finish is paramount. The bare minimum. For others, competing at the Games is a win in itself.

Much has been made of the reduced programme, the city's readiness and the 'what next?' once Glasgow has passed on the hosting baton again, but for now, the attention - finally - is fully on the athletes.

Here, BBC Scotland highlights some of the biggest names set to compete in the upcoming Commonwealth Games.

Is Glasgow ready for the Commonwealth Games - and does the city care?

Can Wales find new Commonwealth legends in Glasgow?

Keep up to date with the Commonwealth Games here

We couldn't start with anyone else, could we?

Even before he walked the walk after talking the talk, new mile world-record holder Josh Kerr was the poster boy for his home Games.

The brazen - arguably gallus - boy from Edinburgh has won the hearts of many Scots, and athletics fans generally, in recent years for his no-nonsense, bullish approach on and off the track.

He set out his stall months ago, targeting the men's mile world record - which had stood for 27 years - at London's Diamond League on Saturday.

Kerr didn't just break it, he smashed it by nearly half a second, running a simply sublime three minutes 42.66 seconds.

The Scot now heads to his homeland for his second Games appearance, competing in the Commonwealth Mile in search of his first medal for Team Scotland.

The 28-year-old told BBC Scotland earlier this year that "gold in Scotland is what I grew up thinking about" and you'd be a brave soul to wager against him when he's in this mood.

Men's one mile final: Scotstoun Stadium on Saturday, 1 August.

It's hard to believe Kadeena Cox has not yet competed at a Commonwealth Games.

The para-cyclist, who won gold in athletics and cycling at the 2016 Paralympics in Rio, has missed the last two Commonwealth Games because of injury.

But the 35-year-old, who was a contestant on I'm A Celebrity... Get Me Out of Here in 2021 after winning Celebrity Masterchef earlier that year, will be hopeful of cooking up a storm at the ever-rowdy Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome for Team England.

In an expanded para-track cycling programme, Cox will compete in the C4-5 event with her sights set on another podium finish.

Cox made the watching world sit up and take notice 10 years ago in Brazil. Sure to be well-backed in Glasgow, it would be right on brand for the vivacious Cox to star in the Glasgow velodrome.

Women's C4-5 4000m individual pursuit final: Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome on Thursday, 30 July.

When it comes to the Commonwealth Games, names don't get much bigger than Chad le Clos.

The South African swimmer is one medal away from becoming the most decorated male athlete in the Games' history. His current haul of 18 - seven gold, four silver and a further seven bronze - is deeply impressive.

A good number of them were collected in Glasgow 12 years ago when he equalled the legendary Ian Thorpe's record of seven medals in one Games.

At 34, Le Clos won't compete in as many races this time but is set to be on the blocks for the men's 50m and 100m butterfly events.

He won gold in the 100m butterfly here back in 2014 and also pocketed a bronze in the 50m - but he claimed a gold in that distance on the Gold Coast in 2018.

Any medal will secure the record for the man from Durban and Glasgow, where he produced such a remarkable show in 2014, is the perfect stage to make history.

Men's 50m butterfly final: Tollcross Swimming Centre on Wednesday, 29 July.

Men's 100m butterfly final: Tollcross Swimming Centre on Sunday, 26 July.

This rising superstar is more a 'if you don't know, get to know' athlete.

Remember the name: Seine Stowers.

A junior Commonwealth Games champion, Stowers marked her 18th birthday earlier this year with four gold medals - breaking 12 records along the way - at the Universal Weightlifting Championship in her homeland of Samoa.

Competing in the women's 77kg - as she will in Glasgow - she lifted 142kg in the clean and jerk category as she established herself as one of Samoa's most exciting medal prospects for years.

Weightlifting runs in the family, too, with her sister, Feagaiga, a gold medal winner on the Gold Coast and a silver medallist in Birmingham four years ago.

Already, though, Seine appears to be taking centre stage.

American Olivia Reeves holds the world record at 77kg in both snatch (123kg) and clean and jerk (155kg), but with Stowers on the scene, who knows for how much longer?

Women's weightlifting (77kg) final: SEC Armadillo on Wednesday, 29 July.

Rhys McClenaghan has done it all.

A European, World, Commonwealth and Olympic champion on the pommel horse, he is undeniably one of the sport's greats, but we've been warned to expect 'Rhys 2.0' in Glasgow.

Northern Ireland's gymnastics golden boy - who secured Ireland's first Olympic gymnastics medal with his gold in Paris two years ago - was forced into a spell on the sidelines last year with a shoulder injury, but he is ready to bring a new routine over to Glasgow.

"The Commonwealth Games is a good chance to show your work in progress. At the Olympic Games, you want to see your finished product," he said.

"It's going to be a work in progress, but hopefully one that is the best in the world too.

"Part of me is striving be like a younger Rhys, but the reality is I'm doing a completely new routine and the rule changes have pushed me into that direction - that's the Rhys 2.0 there."

McClenaghan won gold eight years ago, pipping England's Max Whitlock - who has had to withdraw from these Games with injury - before collecting silver in Birmingham four years ago.

He was the first gymnast to complete the Grand Slam and so arrives at these Games as one of Team NI's wise heads - despite only being 26 years old.

It's a frightening prospect for the rest of the field. Even more frightening is the fact he's rebooting for version 2.0.

Men's pommel horse final: The Arena on Monday, 27 July.

title: Tears and sleepless nights as Marshall and Adlington get Scotland flag honour
description: Bowler Alex Marshall and judoka Sarah Adlington will carry the Scotland flag at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony.
fullText:
There's not much bowler Alex Marshall hasn't experienced in his seven previous Commonwealth Games appearances.

Seven medals, five of them gold. A lifetime of memories. And a gesture that will long hold a place in Scottish sporting folklore.

But while his iconic 'get it up ye' celebration at the end of an epic pairs semi-final with England in 2014 has since been his favourite Games memory, it will be supplanted on Thursday when he carries the Saltire at the opening ceremony.

Perhaps surprisingly, 'Tattie' has only ever hoisted the standard and led the Scotland team in a closing ceremony - last time the Games were in Glasgow 12 years ago.

He has never had the honour of being the opening flagbearer until his Team Scotland colleagues voted that he and judoka Sarah Adlington should share the honour at The Hydro this time around.

Scotland at the Commonwealth Games

Commonwealth Games medal table and schedule

Five big names to look out for at Glasgow 2026

It just adds to what will be an especially special Games for Marshall, who is joined in the bowls team by his niece Beth Riva.

"It's a bit nerve-wracking really," 59-year-old Marshall told BBC Scotland. "It's not very often I get a bit emotional, but I certainly had a tear in my eye when I came out the lift this morning and all the athletes were there. It's just a huge honour."

Resplendent in full kilt on a sweltering day, and proprietorially clutching the flagstick like a child would a favourite toy, the East Lothian bowler was in his element when talking about the news on Wednesday.

Marshall conceded he would have to let it go for the evening before having it returned before the big show.

But he might have to battle with Adlington for first shot of it when that time comes. "And I'd win that fight," joked the 39-year-old heavyweight.

Adlington, who won judo gold in 2014 and 2022 and is looking to end her career by completing the treble, revealed the two of them have known the news for a few weeks but had been sworn to secrecy.

"That's been really hard," she told BBC Sport Scotland. "I was told I could tell my nearest and dearest but for everyone else it will be a massive surprise.

"I'm just so excited. I was so nervous when I woke up this morning, and I think I'll be even more nervous tomorrow. I might not even sleep tonight but if it's even half as good as it was in 2014, it will be amazing."

title: Five Team NI boxers one win from the podium
description: Five Northern Ireland boxers are one win away from a Commonwealth Games' medal following Wednesday's draw.
fullText:
Five Northern Ireland boxers are one win away from a Commonwealth Games' medal following Wednesday's draw.

Kaci Rock, Michaela Walsh, Jansseen Hill, Garyn McAllister and Willie John McCartan will all enter the competition at the quarter-final stage next Tuesday and Wednesday in Glasgow.

Defending featherweight champion Walsh will face Scotland's Lara Brown in Wednesday's afternoon session, as the Holy Family GG boxer seeks to return to the podium for a fourth-straight Games having won silver at the 2014 edition in Glasgow and again four years later at Gold Coast.

Welterweight Rock takes on Niue's Karen Te Ruki Pasene on Tuesday afternoon, which will also see heavyweight McAllister take on Ghana's Jibril Muntari.

In Wednesday's evening session at the SEC Centre, light-heavyweight Hill faces 2025 World bronze medallist Chantelle Reid from England, while super-heavyweight McCartan will meet Ghana's Daniel Plange.

The boxing tournament begins on Friday and bantamweight Louis Rooney is the first Northern Ireland boxer in action, taking on Lesthotho's Mathealira Seholoholo in the opening round at bantamweight and should the Star ABC man prevail, he will meet Australia's Jye Dixon on Sunday in the last 16.

Nicole Clyde begins her bantamweight challenge on Sunday against Ghana's Nancy Bamfo, while welterweight JP Hale is also in action at the least 16 stage with his opponent to be determined.

On Monday, 2022 featherweight champion Jude Gallagher makes his bow when he boxes in the last 16 stage at lightweight with his opponent to be determined, while light-heavyweight Eoghan Quinn is a similar scenario,

Also on Monday's last 16 bouts, flyweight Caitlin Fryers meets Bangladesh's Zinnat Ferdous, while light-middleweight Jon McConnell is tasked with India's Sumit Kundu.

All quarter-finals are scheduled for Tuesday, 28 July and Wednesday, 29 July with Thursday a rest day before the semi-finals on Friday, 31 July and finals on Saturday, 1 August.

title: Programming experience is no longer a moat
description: I have a Computer Science degree. I’ve been writing code for more than 15 years. I’ve written software in more than eight programming languages, read countless programming books, and used to do code golf for fun.
fullText:
I have a Computer Science degree. I’ve been writing code for more than 15
years. I’ve written software in more than eight programming languages, read
countless programming books, and used to do code golf for fun.

Yet in 2026, it feels like I no longer have much of an advantage over someone
who only learned React.

I posted this on X
and asked a simple question: if AI has compressed years of accumulated
knowledge into something anyone can access, what should experienced programmers
focus on now? What becomes the new moat?

Some people read it as a confession rather than a question.

One person, who has been programming since the 1970s,
replied:
“Wow, what an admission.”

Another said
that perhaps I had been a competent code-slinger but had never learned the
important architecture and engineering skills.

The implication was that if AI makes you question the value of your experience,
the only possible explanation is that you never had any. That may be a
comforting answer, but I don’t think it is the correct one.

I have written before that
AI amplifies programmers rather than replacing them.
I still believe that.

Give an incompetent programmer a powerful model and they may produce middling
crap. Give a competent programmer the same model and they can produce excellent
work faster than ever. One of the replies made exactly
this point,
and I agree with it.

Experience helps you notice when generated code is subtly wrong. It helps you
ask better questions, reject bad abstractions, understand trade-offs, and know
when the green tests are lying to you. A beginner sees working code. An
experienced programmer sees the maintenance bill hiding behind it.

That is true, but it still wasn’t the point I was making.

My point is that the distance between incompetence and competence has become
much shorter.

AI does not need to make a beginner equal to an expert to change the economics
of programming. It only needs to help the beginner catch up faster than before,
which is already happening.

When I started programming, knowledge was expensive.

You had to read the book, learn the vocabulary, fight with the compiler, search
through forum posts, misunderstand the documentation, break the program, and
then spend hours figuring out why. A lot of what we call experience is simply
the scar tissue left by repeating this process for years.

Today, a beginner can paste an error into an LLM and get an explanation in
seconds. They can ask for three possible solutions, request a comparison, and
then ask the model to implement the best one. They still need enough knowledge
to judge the result, but the amount they must learn before becoming productive
has collapsed.

The expert still starts further ahead, but the beginner no longer has to walk
the whole road to get somewhere useful.

That is what I mean by compression. AI has not deleted the difference between a
junior and a senior programmer. It has compressed it.

The most interesting reply came from the programmer who disagreed with me. He
said LLMs are
already excellent at systems programming. He routinely gets complicated
compiler tooling from them in one shot, and has had them nearly produce hardware
simulators from a manual in one shot too.

To me, however, this is evidence for my argument rather than against it.

The machine consumed the manual and produced the result. Now imagine that you
need to learn ten manuals. Would you spend months absorbing all of them before
starting, or would you use the machine sitting next to you? I know which option
I would choose.

He later compared
programming by hand to learning arithmetic before using a calculator, solving
integrals before using Mathematica, and operating manual machine tools before
using CNC. I agree with that too. Learning the fundamentals gives you a mental
model that the tool cannot install with a single prompt.

Children should still learn arithmetic, of course, but doing long
multiplication by hand is no longer a moat.

Knowing assembly language can make you a better programmer, but it does not
mean you should manually translate every program into machine code. The
compiler moved that work into a tool, and AI is doing something similar one
level higher.

The knowledge still matters, but the market stops rewarding you merely for
having acquired it the hard way.

This is the uncomfortable part of the discussion, because programming has been
such an important part of how many of us see ourselves.

Typing the same ten things by hand feels mundane when you know a machine can do
them faster. You already proved to yourself that you can write the code. Doing
it again is like reaching for a regular screwdriver when an electric one is
sitting next to it.

You can admire the craftsmanship of the regular screwdriver. You can practice
with it to keep your hands sharp. But don’t confuse that practice with a
competitive advantage.

AI has made code cheaper because, as the models improve and absorb more public
code, documentation, books, and examples, more programming knowledge becomes
available on demand. As a result, the bottleneck moves away from remembering
how to produce the code.

Whether LLMs “truly
think” is an
interesting philosophical argument, but it doesn’t change the practical result.
If a machine can produce the compiler tooling, debug the system, and ship the
feature, the market will care about the result more than the internal mechanism
that produced it.

We will probably live through a turbulent period with mountains of bad
AI-generated software. Vibe coders will ship security holes, broken
abstractions, and dependency piles that nobody understands. The market will
slowly correct for it, though, because users still expect software to work.

Bad output from beginners does not preserve the old moat, though. It simply
shows that better tools have not eliminated the need for judgment.

I don’t think the new moat will be syntax, framework trivia, or remembering an
API that the model can look up in a second. It will be everything around the
code: knowing which problem is worth solving, understanding the users and the
domain, and turning messy requirements into precise decisions.

It will also involve having enough taste to prefer a simple system over an
impressive mess, recognizing when the AI is confidently wrong, and being
willing to own the result after the prompt is finished.

Most of this was always part of good software engineering. The difference is
that it is becoming the job, rather than the layer of thinking around the job.

Experienced programmers should use AI aggressively. We should also keep our
fundamentals sharp, because without them we cannot judge what the machine gives
us. But we should stop treating the difficulty of our education as permanent
protection from newcomers, because it no longer provides that kind of
protection.

AI did not make my 15 years of experience worthless, but it did make a large
part of that experience easier for other people to borrow.

That experience still gives me an advantage, but it no longer feels like the
protective moat it once was. As code becomes cheaper to produce, the judgment
behind it becomes more important, and that is where I think experienced
programmers should focus.

title: China advances plans for single-stack IPv6 network, and surveillance-version
description: IPv6+ could make it easier to censor netizens or block traffic, and Beijing is already exporting it
fullText:
IPv6+ could make it easier to censor netizens or block traffic, and Beijing is already exporting it

China’s Cyberspace Administration on Tuesday issued a plan for wider adoption of IPv6 between now and 2030, and for more work on a non-standard set of services that Beijing calls “IPv6+”.

The Implementation Plan for Deepening Technological Innovation and Integrated Application of Internet Protocol Version 6 (IPv6) (2026-2030) contains the usual promises to increase use of IPv6. Beijing wants 900 million users to be connected over IPv6 by 2027, and for the protocol to carry 38 percent of network traffic. China also wants all connected devices to be IPv6-enabled by 2027.

“By 2030, IPv6 will be widely and deeply integrated with all sectors of the economy and society, building a technologically advanced, open, innovative, self-driven, and secure IPv6 industrial ecosystem,” the regulator wrote. “The number of active IPv6 users will reach 950 million, and IPv6 will account for 42 percent of network traffic.”

The Administration also expects that by 2030, “New networks will be prioritized for IPv6 addresses by default, accelerating the evolution towards IPv6 single-stack, and promoting the formation of a network service and application system dominated by IPv6.”

News that China is planning for the day it runs a single-stack internet will excite some, notwithstanding the fact that IPv6 has proven less important than its creators hoped.

The most interesting part of the new plan is the call for more work on IPv6+ – a set of enhancements to IPv6 that allows those who send information to embed metadata describing content into packets and even suggest the route it should take.

As Think Tank the Mercator Institute for China Studies last year observed, IPv6+ “has obvious appeal for authoritarian regimes looking to control their citizens,” because a carrier could read metadata and act on it.

One possible action could be to allow network operators to identify traffic they would like to charge extra to carry, an idea telcos like because they feel it is unfair that they bear the burden of investing in last-mile infrastructure to deliver content from the likes of Netflix and YouTube.

Reading metadata could also enable censorship: Beijing already blocks a lot of content, and if dissidents had to identify themselves in packets, they’d be easier to find and block.

The Institute also notes that China’s telco equipment companies have implemented IPv6+ and exported kit that runs it to several nations.

That’s worrying because China already tried to create a protocol called “New IP” that also included surveillance-friendly features.

China tried to have the International Telecommunications Union sign off on New IP, despite the Internet Engineering Task Force maintaining existing IP protocols. That effort failed, but Beijing is pressing ahead with its efforts to spread its own version of IP at home and abroad.

The new plan doesn’t suggest that Beijing abandon vanilla IPv6. Indeed, it calls for Chinese participation in global standards development.

But the document also says China will work on “national IPv6 standards and accelerate the development of national IPv6 standards in key areas.” ®

title: Durable Objects Are Made for Agents
fullText:
For the last few months, I've been building almost exclusively with Cloudflare Durable Objects (DOs). They are a wonderful primitive for building products (particularly agents), and I wish more providers out there had a similar offering. There really isn't anything like them in the market.

As I was comparing notes with various friends, I realized that most people haven't really even heard of DOs, and don't understand why they are such a big deal. It's a shame because they really are a near-perfect fit for building agents on top of them.

I won't gloss over the fact that there are shortcomings with DOs, and I wanted to share some thoughts about where they shine as well as where they fall short.

Disclosure: I am long a small amount of NET and am very bullish on their prospects.

Durable Objects combine a few simple concepts...

Rather than thinking about your code as a bunch of services which talk to a database, you start thinking about your service as a set of many 'objects' which are event driven by new requests or by timers you can set (called alarms). Each one gets an ID, and requests are routed accordingly to a single isolate by ID. To see what I mean, here's what a simplified version of a Chat Room looks like:

Each ChatRoom instance then handles its own state: it accepts websockets and broadcasts every message to everyone connected to that room:

Once you start thinking this way... you start wanting to make everything a DO.

Sticking with the chat metaphor... suppose you are spinning up a Slack clone (as is trendy to talk about these days). You might lay out your data model like this:

When a user loads Slack, they typically want some sort of notification feed on the workspace, and a realtime feed of a channel. Sending a message to a channel should be ordered. You typically don't query across channels (except for a search use case, which can hit a vector store). If you wanted to go nuts you could even have specific ThreadDOs, or UserDOs, or any sort of DB you don't need to JOIN against.

Starting with the beginning of the dev lifecycle, an extremely underrated part of working with DOs is their local dev setup. Locally, you run your services using wrangler. It's a CLI that functions as a local env + all-in-one toolkit for working with Cloudflare.

I initially was a bit weirded out by this, but using wrangler for local dev is actually quite nice. It becomes really quick to test changes when your coding agent doesn't rely on Docker or Postgres. Because it's all running SQLite in prod, the production drift for connecting to a DB is fairly minimal.

DOs also run explicitly in a single-threaded manner. That means you have to worry less about locking and concurrency. You know that only one invocation of your DO is running against its storage at a time.

Durable Objects also come with native support for websockets. This is a godsend for anyone who has spent a long time thinking about fan-out for large-scale notification feeds. Rather than having some sort of large multi-tiered fanout system, you just keep connections warm per-DO, and then broadcast to any connected listeners. It's perfect for building agents or Slack-like experiences.

Binding to other DOs, services, or R2 buckets all feels much more intuitive vs what I'd see with AWS or GCP. You simply specify the bindings in the wrangler.jsonc configuration, and then you get the ability to talk to the service. No need to think about complex network rules, VPCs, Security Groups, or NAT ingress.

Preview environments are ~close to free. Since DOs are cheap and don't cost anything while idle, you can just tell your agent to create new versions in CI on every single branch and deploy and then clean them up later.

As a last bonus, I find working with Cloudflare infra to be extremely token efficient. To build a chat room implementation, you only require a few lines of code. Like humans, coding agents that have to explore the sprawl across a large codebase tend to slow way way down.

If you do decide to go the DO route and don't really care much about the internals, I'd recommend using either Think or Flue. Both make a lot of fairly sane choices in my opinion.

I said before that DOs had an amazing concurrency model because they are single-threaded. This is true, but you can't just straight up ignore the complexity either. There's a good blog post on this which goes into detail, but the gist is that some functions get marked as having readable/writable gates. If you aren't careful, some part of your function may end up sampling from an LLM before writing to storage; and that hangs all your subscribers! I hit a few cases where agent-generated code would end up blocking read requests for 10s of seconds at a time.1

I also hit a few reliability issues early on that I just had no ability to go and fix. Stuff like misconfigured gateways to cloud LLM APIs, or inability to deploy via the Cloudflare APIs. These weren't necessarily DO-specific things, but I felt like I wasn't getting the same level of observability or control that I might from AWS/GCP/Azure.

If you decide to use DOs, the first-class support is all Typescript. In general I'm a fan of Typescript, but you should know that if you want to work in Rust, Golang, or Python, you will have fewer options available and fewer examples.

I ended up just using my own types rather than the Wrangler typegen. For a long time I couldn't figure out how to get them working properly, before I just gave in and had all services declare interfaces/contracts.

Every so often, missing parts of the Node APIs can come and bite you. I got hit a few times because HTTP/2 isn't supported for things like gRPC to the Modal APIs. This makes some sense, since a worker can't just hang for a long time, but it does make you have to be careful about library usage, especially around native deps.

Migrations and versioning also get a bit trickier. Typically the way you change data shape within the DO is by adding some migration code when the DO wakes. This works pretty well, but it's easy to lose track of "what's the current state" of the schema, especially if you didn't wake every single DO. You can effectively never remove the old migration paths, unless you go through and wake all DOs.

Lastly, many of my potential customers want the ability to BYO-Cloud. This is pretty understandable if you're building a product which deals with sensitive data, and Cloudflare makes it a bit of a non-starter.

I mentioned before that no other cloud seems to have a similar offering. While that's true, I do think there are a few early but interesting options.

Restate provides a cheap durable execution runtime, similar to a faster / cheaper Temporal. It's built by the ex-Flink team so it leans heavily into a log structure for handling events and replaying. Restate has a concept of 'Virtual Objects', which provide many of the same semantics as a Durable Object: single writer, attached storage, etc. You can self-host Restate, and it has bindings in other languages like Rust and Golang (disclosure: I am a small-time angel investor, and am a fan of what they are doing). I've started using it as my durable-execution-runtime on AWS.

Rivet is a new YC company that provides infrastructure they refer to as 'actors'. Spiritually it feels very similar to a lot of what Cloudflare is building—they lean heavily into V8 isolates. I haven't seriously evaluated them, but from what I can tell on the outside, they seem to be doing a lot of the right things.

In terms of other primitives I think are promising, Vercel leads the pack. Workflows, Sandboxes, AI Gateway, Vercel Connect, and Functions all create usable primitives which you can string together to build these same sorts of long-lived agents. EVE feels like the culmination of all of these tools, composed in a straightforward manner.

I hit this issue and wanted to prefer correctness over throughput, which ended up with the ctx.blockConcurrencyWhile approach (blocking the gates). The good news is it's since been fixed. ↩

title: Grimoire – Best Practices for Everyone Installed for Your AI Agents
description: 🧙 Grimoire is a free, open-source package manager for best practices — 1000+ skills across 26 domains (engineering, law, finance, design, health, and more), each sourced from a real institution or ...
fullText:
The world's knowledge is in your AI. The world's practice is not.
A modern skills manager for AI agents — install, manage, and enforce best practices the way npm manages packages.

"We're launching our SaaS in 48 hours. I'm terrified something will break. What do we do?"

Not an engineer? See Skills in Action for a sample, or browse all 27 domains in grimoire-core.

Your AI knows everything — Grimoire makes it practice it.

Books gave everyone knowledge. Google gave everyone access. AI gave everyone comprehension. None of them gave everyone practice. Grimoire does.

The model knows SOLID, DDD, Google SRE, and the ABA Model Rules. Without explicit guidance, it enforces none of them. Grimoire is the enforcement layer — skills: named, citable, executable units of expert practice, one concept, one source, one set of steps.

If you've spent 10,000 hours mastering something, your practice belongs here.

Every AI coding tool ships its own config format — CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, AGENTS.md. Developers copy-paste and drift. Grimoire solves this with a package manager model: declare the practices you need in grimoire.toml, pin versions in grimoire.lock, install to every agent in one command.

Any git repo is a valid package. grimoire-core is the official one — curated, cited, peer-reviewed. Tools like Tessl distribute skills broadly. Grimoire adds the enforcement layer: curated expert practices, version-locked, with grimoire check to close the loop from declaration to compliance.

Ref syntax: owner/repo[@tag][:path] — the :path suffix is a Standard Glob pattern (doublestar) matched against each skill's domain path. ** matches any depth; * matches within one segment. Examples: engineering/** (all engineering skills), health/sleep/** (one subdomain only), **/development/** (development subdomain in any domain).

grimoire-core is the official package — curated, cited, and reviewed against STANDARD.md. But grimoire manages any package without restriction. Mix and match.

Community packages install identically to grimoire-core. We encourage following the grimoire skill standard for quality and interoperability — but any git repo works. Publish as grimoire-<name> for discoverability:

The practices in your installed packages define what grimoire check validates against — declare the package, get the linter rules for free.

Declare which practices your project follows. grimoire check enforces them — CI gate, watch mode, or on-demand.

Gate any PR: grimoire check --junit report.xml exits non-zero when coverage drops below the configured threshold. No other tool checks whether your project follows its declared practices — only whether the config file is valid.

Engineering — propose a commit message from staged diff:

Every skill is structured: triggering condition, verified source, numbered steps, no freeform guessing.

Or describe the situation in plain language — you don't need to know which skill applies:

suggest-best-practice auto-classifies any situation, routes to the matching skill,
or tells you exactly what to install if the skill isn't in your library yet.

→ See skills across all 27 domains — law, finance, health, cooking, and more

Then run the interactive wizard (all platforms):

Auto-detects Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenClaw, and OpenCode.

Native plugin shortcuts (Claude Code):

Granular installs (within a package):

Or via plugin in opencode.json:

OpenClaw: see .openclaw/INSTALL.md or run grimoire install --target openclaw.

Or natively via agy plugin install https://github.com/jeffreytse/grimoire-core. Skills land in ~/.gemini/config/skills (global) or .agent/skills (project-scoped); the trigger line is written to ~/.gemini/AGENTS.md.

grimoire lsp implements the Language Server Protocol over stdio. Any LSP-capable editor gets compliance diagnostics in the gutter in real time — no plugin required beyond a one-time language-client config.

How it works: on every file save the server runs grimoire check in the background and pushes findings as LSP diagnostics to your editor. Pass/hint items are suppressed; only errors, warnings, and info appear.

VSCode (settings.json — requires grimoire extension or a custom extension):

New to grimoire? Run the interactive wizard:

The wizard walks you through registry selection, domain choice, and agent linking — no manual config editing. It detects your installed AI agents and installs the right skills automatically.

After install, describe any problem in plain language:

For CI enforcement, initialize once per project and gate PRs with the grimoire CLI:

New to grimoire? Start with /suggest-best-practice. Describe any professional or life situation — it reads your context and routes you to the matching skill, or tells you exactly what to install if the skill isn't in your library yet.

→ BPDD guide — cycle, compliance linter, LSP output, suppression, CI integration

Describe any problem → suggest-best-practice

Describe any situation. suggest-best-practice reads it, finds the best-matching skill(s), and routes you there.

Multiple practices needed → plan-best-practice-solution

When a problem needs 2+ practices coordinated in sequence — within one domain or across many — plan-best-practice-solution decomposes it with MECE methodology, sequences skills by dependency, and recursively plans complex sub-problems.

Catching gaps before you start → start-best-practice

Before any task begins, start-best-practice intercepts, surfaces the relevant practices you should apply, and applies them — so you enter the work already aligned instead of discovering gaps mid-execution.

Opaque complex problem → apply-best-practice-tree

When sub-problems are unknown until you start solving — apply-best-practice-tree builds a dynamic execution tree, discovering and applying practices as the structure emerges.

Internalizing a practice → learn-best-practice

When you want to actually own a practice — not just apply it once — learn-best-practice runs active recall, spaced-repetition review, and scenario drilling so you can use it without AI in the future.

When top-tier orgs disagree (Google vs IBM, startup vs enterprise), pin which approach you prefer once — grimoire applies it automatically from then on.

Override priority — grimoire checks preferences in this order, first match wins:

Configure manually — edit the settings files directly without going through the AI:

Project configuration overrides global. Session pins override both. Teams can share a global standard while individual projects deviate where needed.

practices = ["OOP"] vs profiles = ["oop"] — both signal OOP intent, but differently. practices = ["OOP"] in a domain section is a loose hint — the AI leans toward OOP conventions from its training. profiles = ["oop"] at the top level activates specific installed skills (exact steps, validated sources). Use profiles for precision; practices for domain-level style preference. → Full comparison

→ Full configuration reference — all keys, override hierarchy, TOML examples

Activate a named set of skills in one line — no list to maintain, no file to create.

Grimoire resolves the name in this order, first match wins:

If no file or inline definition exists, the tag query fires automatically. profiles = ["oop"] works without creating any file.

Multiple profiles — combine paradigms; first entry wins conflicts, duplicates are deduplicated:

Custom profile — curate a team-specific subset when the tag set is too broad:

Commit .grimoire/profiles/ to share standards across the team. Publish as a gist or repo (grimoire-profile-<name>) for the community.

Inline profile — define directly in grimoire.toml, no separate file:

If a profiles/my-team.toml file also exists for the same name, the file wins.

profiles vs practices — profiles activates skill bundles globally; practices is a domain-scoped explicit list. → Full comparison

→ Full profiles guide — resolution order, conflict handling, sharing profiles

grimoire is a linter for best practices — same model as ESLint for code style:

Declare which practices you require in grimoire.toml, install the packages that encode them, then run grimoire check against any artifact — a codebase, a legal contract, a business plan, a training program. Same criteria every run. Gaps that survive human review get caught by the check.

The cycle — same inversion as TDD: declare what "good" looks like first, then bring the artifact into alignment.

Run /apply-best-practice-driven-development to drive the full cycle. Run /check-best-practice-compliance for a one-off check.

Two modes — choose based on how you want to run the AI:

Output — always written to .grimoire/reports/:

The JSON follows the LSP Diagnostic schema — uri + range locate any finding in any text artifact, not just code.

Coverage thresholds — set in grimoire.toml, enforced on every check:

Use /fix-best-practice-finding to fix one specific compliance finding — targeted, location-aware, verified. Use /apply-best-practice-driven-development to fix everything systematically.

→ Full BPDD guide — cycle, linter, LSP schema, false positive suppression, incremental mode

grimoire-core maintains an open standard for AI agent skill quality — freely adoptable by any skill library. Community packages are encouraged to follow it; grimoire installs any git repo regardless. This quality gate applies to grimoire-core contributions only.

Every skill must pass review-best-practice-skill before merge:

grimoire validate is the reference implementation of the standard — a static linter for SKILL.md files:

Checks run on every skill: required frontmatter fields, tag format, description quality, name uniqueness, and semantic near-duplicate detection across the library.

→ Read the full standard · Adopt this standard

See CONTRIBUTING.md to submit a skill.

Domains below are from grimoire-core, the official package. Community packages may cover any domain structure — grimoire imposes no layout.

📦 Official package: github.com/jeffreytse/grimoire-core

Isn't this already in the model's training data?

Yes — and no. Models know about best practices. Skills make models do them, reliably.

For simple tasks (write a test, fix a bug), the skeptic is right — the model doesn't need a skill. For complex, multi-step workflows — an SLO design, a post-mortem, an incident response — skills measurably change what you get. The model knows Google's engineering review process exists. It does not reliably know which question to ask first, what the output format is, or when to stop. That's what a skill encodes.

These are just textbook practices the model already knows. Why bother?

Knowing a practice and reliably executing it are different things. Ask any model "I just had a production incident" — you'll get a generic write-up. Run write-post-mortem and you get: blameless framing, 5-whys, timeline, contributing factors, action items with owners, and a detection section. The model knew all of that before the skill existed. The skill is what makes it happen consistently, in the right format, every time.

The "textbook" objection gets it backwards. Established practices are ideal for skills precisely because they're falsifiable — you can verify whether the output matches what Google's SRE book, Amazon's mechanisms, or the WHO protocol actually prescribes. If you find a skill that adds nothing over a bare prompt, that's a quality failure. File an issue.

Some frameworks here are universally known — isn't the value already in the model?

The question isn't whether the model knows the framework name. It's whether the skill encodes what practitioners who already know the name still get wrong.

A framework qualifies when the skill has substantial content beyond the acronym or label — the step most people skip, the failure mode they don't avoid, the discipline that separates expert application from surface-level application. SWOT, for example, is universally known, but most practitioners stop at the 4-quadrant list and never derive the TOWS cross-matrix (SO/WO/ST/WT strategies) — the step that converts a diagnosis into actionable options. The skill encodes that gap.

A framework doesn't qualify when the full implementation reduces to restating the framework name. If a skill's entire content would be "follow the acronym," it adds nothing — the model already knows the letters.

The test: "What would this skill contain beyond the framework name?" If the answer is "the step practitioners skip + the failure mode they don't avoid" — it qualifies. If the answer is "the letters, explained" — it doesn't.

Does grimoire conflict with my team's existing conventions?

Skills describe what the world's top institutions do. Your team may do things differently — and be right to. Two ways to handle it:

Pin your preference. Tell grimoire which approach to follow when practices conflict:

Override or fork. A skill is a starting point, not a mandate. Adapt any skill to your context, or ignore it entirely. The format is plain Markdown and the license is MIT.

Add a skill — share expert knowledge across law, finance, engineering, cooking, or any of the 27 domains. First skill takes ~30 minutes.

→ Contributing guide in grimoire-core

Improve the CLI — bug fixes, new commands, performance, documentation.

→ Open issues · CONTRIBUTING.md

grimoire is free. It replaces $500/hr lawyers, $300 doctor visits, and $1M McKinsey
engagements — at zero cost, forever.

If it saved you time, money, or a bad decision:

Every star makes grimoire more visible. Every sponsorship funds one more domain.

This project is licensed under the MIT license © Jeffrey Tse.

title: Villa agree loan deal for Chelsea winger Garnacho
description: Aston Villa agree a loan deal for Chelsea winger Alejandro Garnacho until the end of the 2026-27 season, with an obligation to buy.
fullText:
Aston Villa have agreed a loan deal for Chelsea winger Alejandro Garnacho until the end of the 2026-27 season, with an obligation to buy.

The 22-year-old has already completed a medical in Birmingham and has pre-agreed a four-year contract at Villa Park if the loan deal becomes permanent.

The terms of the obligation to buy clause has not been disclosed by either club, but it is expected to be triggered if certain conditions are met, such as the number of appearances the Argentina international makes next season.

Chelsea believe the terms are easily reachable and that they will achieve their original £43m valuation.

Garnacho's departure from Stamford Bridge follows Chelsea's signing of Morgan Rogers for a record £117m fee from Villa on Tuesday.

The winger becomes Villa's fourth signing, after the arrivals of Johan Manzambi, Joao Gomes and Modou Keba Cisse.

Villa's player sales this summer, meanwhile, total nearly £200m, with Youri Tielemans, Donyell Malen, Enzo Barrenechea and Lewis Dobbin all leaving.

The Blues have been keen to offload Garnacho despite signing him only last September in a £40m deal from Manchester United, who would be due 10% of any eventual transfer fee.

His impact at Stamford Bridge has been limited to one Premier League goal, and the arrival of Rogers and Sporting winger Geovany Quenda have increased the need to reduce the size of the squad.

Garnacho, Rogers and two clubs trying to balance the books

Garnacho's only season at Chelsea did not go to plan, a fact perhaps best illustrated by that solitary Premier League goal.

In his defence, he started only nine matches following Liam Rosenior's appointment, a 29-game period that also included Calum McFarlane's second spell as interim manager at the end of the season.

Across all competitions, Garnacho scored a more respectable eight goals and provided four assists in 43 appearances last season.

However, aside from two goals against Arsenal in the Carabao Cup, his other goals came against lesser opposition, including Qarabag, Wrexham, Port Vale and Cardiff City.

It also became apparent that Garnacho was beginning to lose the support of some supporters because of a perceived lack of work-rate.

There were also occasions when he was criticised for failing to track his runner, including during a home defeat by West Ham in which he was substituted at half-time after being involved in the build-up to two goals.

Sources have told BBC Sport that Garnacho remained professional and worked hard behind the scenes, which bodes well for his future.

In a late-season interview with Premier League Productions, the former Manchester United winger admitted he had done some "bad things" at Old Trafford because he was not playing enough.

Others familiar with Chelsea dressing room dynamics said Garnacho struggled to integrate fully into the group and often cut a distant figure.

However, there is no significant controversy surrounding his departure from Stamford Bridge. The Blues simply viewed him as a player they were willing to move on as part of efforts to improve the squad.

Subscribe and listen for everything you love about Villa

Latest Aston Villa news, analysis and fan views

Ask about Aston Villa - what do you want to know?

title: De-complect: Skill to remove braided complexity from projects
description: Removes "complections" which weave together unnecessary aspects of the code - danverbraganza/de-complect
fullText:
An Agent Skill that helps AI coding agents remove braided complexity from design documents and
implementation branches — the way Rich Hickey's Simple Made Easy means simple: one fold,
un-braided, not "familiar" or "fewer files."

This repository provides an Agent Skill for Claude Code and other compatible AI agents. It teaches
the agent to find where independent concerns are twisted together — defensive checks, sentinels,
tri-state configs, scattered validation, speculative abstraction, stored-but-derivable state — and
cut those braids so each concern hangs straight down.

It runs on two kinds of target:

This is a quality pass, not a bug hunt. It preserves the document's intent and scope, and
preserves code behavior; genuine correctness bugs are surfaced separately, never fixed here.

This downloads the skill into ~/.claude/skills/de-complect/. Override the destination with
CLAUDE_SKILLS_DIR.

The skill follows the standard skills/<name>/SKILL.md layout, so skill managers discover it
straight from the repo:

For the agent-skills-manager dashboard, open Install → From Git and paste
https://github.com/danverbraganza/de-complect, then pick de-complect.

The repo doubles as its own single-plugin marketplace. From inside Claude Code:

Then reload (/reload-plugins) and invoke the skill with /de-complect:de-complect.

Copy the skill directory into your skills directory — either global:

Once installed, the skill activates when you ask an agent to "simplify", "de-complect", "untangle",
"remove cruft", "make erroneous states unrepresentable", or "clean up" a design doc or a branch —
or when a diff is thick with defensive checks, sentinels, and stored-but-derivable state.

Contributions are welcome. Keep the skill a quality pass — disentangling, behavior-preserving —
and route correctness findings to a dedicated bug-hunting review.

title: Liverpool confident of signing Barcola - Thursday's gossip
description: Alex Scott favours a move to Arsenal, Bruno Fernandes is ready to commit his future to Manchester United, Liverpool are confident they will sign Bradley Barcola, plus more.
fullText:
Liverpool are confident of signing Bradley Barcola, Alex Scott favours a move to Arsenal, Bruno Fernandes is ready to commit his future to Manchester United.

Liverpool are confident they can beat Arsenal and Bayern Munich to the signature of Paris St-Germain forward Bradley Barcola, after the 23-year-old impressed with four goal contributions at the World Cup with France this summer. (Sun), external

Arsenal are edging the battle with Manchester United for sought-after Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott, 22 - but the England Under-21 international has concerns around how much game time he would get at the Emirates. (Mirror), external

Scott is keen on a move to Chelsea after the Cherries rejected a £64m bid from the Blues. (The Standard), external

Portugal midfielder Bruno Fernandes, 31, has decided to commit his future to Manchester United, with talks around a new deal ongoing. (Express), external

Besiktas are trying to sign English midfielder Joe Willock, 26, who is in the final year of his contract at Newcastle and will not be offered a new deal. (Telegraph), external

Arsenal are not ruling out the sale of their captain Martin Odegaard, and will listen to offers for the 27-year-old Norway midfielder. (Teamtalk), external

Manchester United are exploring a move for Real Madrid midfielder Eduardo Camavinga, 23, who missed out on a place in France's World Cup squad this summer. (Nicolo Schira), external

Roma have asked about the availability of Chelsea centre-back Axel Disasi, 28 - and the Blues have set an asking price of around £17m for the Frenchman. (Footmercato - in French), external

Fulham have opened negotiations with Real Madrid over a potential move for highly-rated striker Gonzalo Garcia, 22. (The Athletic - subscription required), external

Captain Salt leads Fire to victory against Brave
description: Welsh Fire captain Phil Salt leads from the front as his unbeaten 47 helps them begin the 2026 Hundred season with a six-wicket win against Southern Brave in Southampton.
fullText:
The Hundred men's competition, Utilita Bowl

Southern Brave 135-7 (100 balls): Miller 44 (35); Jansen 2-17

Welsh Fire 138-4 (98 balls): Salt 47* (37); Chaudhary 2-19

Welsh Fire captain Phil Salt led from the front as his unbeaten 47 helped them begin the 2026 Hundred season with a six-wicket win against Southern Brave in Southampton.

Former world number one-ranked T20 batter Salt is The Hundred's all-time leading scorer, having hit 1,138 runs in five seasons with his former side Manchester Originals.

Having signed for Fire as their captain before this year's competition, he is aiming to guide the Welsh franchise, who finished bottom of both the men's and women's table last season, to their first-ever title.

Chasing 136 on Wednesday, Salt played the supporting role early on as fellow opener Matt Short struck a brisk 20 before he kept the visitors on course through partnerships with Joe Root (14) and Rachin Ravindra (24).

With nine runs required from the final five balls, Asa Tribe hit Brave captain Chris Jordan's first ball for four before Salt sealed the victory with a six two balls later.

Batting first, Brave lost openers Jamie Smith and Ben McKinney for single figures but 18-year-old Thomas Rew helped rebuild their innings with a flashy 41 from 30 balls.

The Fire bowlers, led by Marco Jansen who finished with 2-17, kept the scoring in check for much of the innings.

David Miller struggled to find his rhythm at the start of his innings but finished strongly for Brave before departing in the penultimate delivery with a 35-ball 44, which lifted the hosts to 135-7, which ultimately proved not enough.

Brave fightback against Fire to make winning start

The Welsh players aiming to light up The Hundred

Salt's path home from Barbados to Welsh Fire captain

London Spirit will take on Manchester Super Giants at Lord's on Thursday, with the women's game starting at 14:45 BST and the men's at 18:30.

You can follow ball-by-ball commentary on BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra, BBC Sounds and the BBC Sport website and app.

Saliba to miss extended period with back injury
description: Arsenal confirm defender William Saliba is set to miss an "extended period" after suffering a back injury while at the World Cup with France.
fullText:
Arsenal have confirmed defender William Saliba is set to miss an "extended period" after suffering a back injury while at the World Cup with France.

The 25-year-old had been managing a back issue for several weeks and carried the problem into the World Cup but was forced off after 30 minutes of France's semi-final loss to Spain.

After returning to London and being seen by a specialist, it was decided that surgery is not needed with a managed recovery programme to be commenced instead.

Saliba was forced to sit out France's World Cup bronze final defeat by England.

Earlier in the World Cup, he said: "I've had some minor niggles for several months.

"I've been gritting my teeth because there was the Champions League and the Premier League.

"The World Cup comes round only once every four years, so you've got to grit your teeth."

Saliba made 50 appearances across all competitions for Arsenal last season, as they ended a 22-year wait to win the Premier League and also reached the Champions League final.

Mikel Arteta's side will take on Manchester City in the Community Shield on Sunday, 16 August - five days before the Gunners begin the defence of the Premier League title, hosting promoted Coventry City.

title: Miami mistakenly post James 'introductory' video
description: Miami Heat mistakenly post a link on YouTube to LeBron James' 'introductory press conference' despite the NBA great not yet deciding where he will play next season.
fullText:
Miami Heat mistakenly posted a link on YouTube to LeBron James' 'introductory press conference' despite the NBA great not yet deciding where he will play next season.

The link to the live stream - scheduled for 27 July - was uploaded to the Heat's channel on Tuesday before being deleted.

A Heat spokesperson told Reuters, external the video was posted in error by staff who were preparing for the possibility of James joining the side.

BBC Sport has contacted Miami Heat for comment.

James, 41, announced he was leaving the Los Angeles Lakers in June after eight years, in which he led them to the 2020 NBA title - his fourth overall.

He previously played for Miami between 2010 and 2014.

James averaged 20.9 points, 7.2 assists and 6.1 rebounds last season with the Lakers.

The 22-time All-Star is the NBA's all-time leader in games (1,622) and points (43,440).

A return to Florida for James would form a formidable starting five for the Heat alongside fellow new signing and two-time MVP Giannis Antetokounmpo and centre Bam Adebayo.

Given the Heat traded key members of their squad away to land Antetokounmpo from the Milwaukee Bucks, a player of James' calibre would add further scoring power and experience with the Miami team focused on a quick rebuild towards a title push.

But earlier this week, James' agent Rich Paul said he is "not going to be rushed" into making a decision.

"It's his choice to make and when he makes the choice, he'll make it," Paul said on the Game Over with Max Kellerman and Rich Paul podcast.

LeBron's Last Dance - what next for James after Lakers exit?
